segments.s167-g473: This is a nominal phrase: یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain' and precedes the noun باد (wind), a typical literary order. با + تیره گردی سیاه is a prepositional phrase 'with a dark black dust-cloud' where تیره modifies گردی and سیاه adds an extra adjective; stacking adjectives like this is a poetic, emphatic description.
segments.s167-g474: برآمد is a simple past/preterite 'arose' (third-person) describing the wind's coming. بپوشید is a past action 'covered' formed with the archaic/poetic prefix b- plus the past stem (here functioning as the verb that follows برآمد); خورشید و ماه are the direct objects ('the sun and the moon') placed after the verb without را, a normal pattern in classical Persian.
segments.s167-g475: همی is an adverb here indicating a continued or repeated action ('still' / continuous), followed by یکدگر را 'one another' marked by the object marker را. ندیدند is the past negative 'they did not see' (ن- + past stem + -ند for third-person plural). The noun روی 'face' is left at the segment end because it links syntactically to the verb گرفتند in the next segment (روی گرفتند = 'they turned their faces').
segments.s167-g476: گرفتند is the main past verb 'they took/seized' (here part of the compound idea 'روی گرفتند' = 'they turned their faces' or more generally 'they took/assumed ...'). نفرین همه is the noun phrase 'the curse of all / everyone's curse' where همه quantifies نفرین, and بر introduces the target 'upon' which is گروی. گروی here functions as the noun denoting the one who has 'joined' or 'adopted' (a verbal root used nominally), so the whole phrase means 'they laid everyone's curse upon that joiner/renegade.'
segments.s167-g449: سیاوش بدو گفت پدرود باش is structured as Subject (سیاوش) + indirect object/adverbial (بدو = 'to him') + verb (گفت = 'said') followed by the quoted imperative/farewell (پدرود باش). پدرود باش is an optative/imperative formula meaning 'be farewell' or 'farewell to you' (پدرود as the noun 'farewell' with باش as the imperative/optative).
segments.s167-g450: زمین تار و تو جاودان پود باش is a poetic imperative/blessing: the first noun (زمین 'earth') is linked to تار ('warp' in weaving) and the second phrase addresses 'you' (تو) with جاودان پود ('eternal weft'), followed by the imperative باش ('be'). Word order is nominal + predicative noun phrase + باش as the verb-like optative marker.
segments.s167-g451: درودی ز من سوی پیران رسان is an imperative instruction: درودی ('a greeting') + ز من ('from me') + سوی پیران ('to the elders') and the verb رسان (imperative/infinitive form of 'to deliver/bring'). The postpositional/z-preposition ز marks origin ('from'), and سوی marks direction/recipient.
segments.s167-g452: بگویش که گیتی دگر شد بسان is a sequence of clauses: بگویش ('tell him/them') + that-clause introduced by که. Inside the that-clause, گیتی ('the world') is subject, دگر ('different/other') is predicate modifier and شد is the past/complete verb 'became/has become'; بسان ('as/so') is a final comparative/adverbial particle reinforcing 'changed'.
segments.s167-g453: به پیران نه زین‌گونه بودم امید uses the dative/prepositional به ('toward/for') with پیران ('the elders') to indicate the target of expectation. نه زین‌گونه ('not in this way') negates the following predicate; بودم is past-tense 'I was' (here part of the idiom 'I had hope' or 'I expected'), and امید is the noun 'hope/expectation' serving as the main predicate/complaint.
segments.s167-g454: همی پند او باد بد من چو بید is an optative/cursing clause: همی ('always') modifies پند او ('his counsel/advice'); باد is the optative particle meaning 'may it be', followed by بد ('bad/ill') and the beneficiary/experiencer من ('me'). چو بید ('like a willow') is a simile. Overall: 'May his counsel always be bad for me, like a willow.'
segments.s167-g455: مرا گفته بود او که با صد هزار functions as reported speech framing: مرا (me) + گفته بود (had told) + او (he) + که (that) + با ('with'). Here مرا is the object marker combined with the pronoun (written split in the data), گفته بود is pluperfect/past perfect 'had said/told', and که introduces the content clause. The given tokenization splits مرا into parts that do not match listed senses, so some entries do not align directly with the grammar.
segments.s167-g456: زره‌دار و برگستوان‌ور سوار is a noun phrase giving the accompaniment: زره‌دار ('armored warrior/armored') + و ('and') + برگستوان‌ور (an adjective-like compound meaning 'mighty/strong-armed') modifying سوار ('rider/horseman'). The sequence lists attributes then the head noun (سوار).
segments.s167-g273: چنین گفت is a fixed formula meaning "thus said." سالار توران سپاه is a nominal compound (no ezāfe shown) meaning "the commander of the army of Turan" ( literally: "commander [of the] Turan army"). Word order is formulaic: speech marker + verb + title + place/force.
segments.s167-g274: که introduces the following content/relative clause ("that / who / which"). ایدر = "here." کشیدش is past transitive کشیدن with the object suffix -ش = "he/they pulled him." به یکسو = "to one side/towards one side." ز راه = poetic prepositional phrase "from the road." Together: "that here pulled him aside from the road."
segments.s167-g275: کنیدش is an imperative (2nd-person plural) of کردن with the object suffix -ش = "do it to him" → here "cut him". به خنجر = instrument phrase "with a dagger" (به + instrument). سر از تن جدا is the idiomatic phrasing "separate the head from the body" (literally: "head from body apart").
segments.s167-g276: به here again marks manner/instrument ("by/with"). شخی is a nominalized/poetic word from شخ (a violent snap/blow) meaning "a smashing stroke." که introduces a result/relative clause "such that / so that." هرگز نروید گیا = "may never grow (any) plant" or "no plant ever grows" — نروید is classical/formal present/subjunctive of روییدن (to grow), and گیا = "plant/vegetation."
segments.s167-g277: بریزید is the imperative (2nd-person plural) "pour/scatter." خونش = خون + -ش (his blood) is the direct object. بران is an archaic/prepositional form meaning "upon that/thereon" (بر + آن contracted). گرم خاک = "warm earth/soil" with adjective before noun (poetic word order).
segments.s167-g278: ممانید is an exhortative/negative form meaning "do not remain/stay" (2nd-person plural). دیر = adverb "long/for long" so ممانید دیر = "do not stay long." و = "and." مدارید here functions as a hortative/negative command meaning roughly "do not (be)" or "do not show" (classical/poetic usage) and governs باک ("fear"), so مدارید باک = "do not have fear / be not fearful."
segments.s167-g279: چنین گفت = "thus said." با شاه here means "to/with the king" (classical texts use با to indicate address/interaction). یکسر سپاه = "the entire army at once" (یکسر modifies سپاه = whole/entire). Word order: speech marker + verb + indirect object/recipient + modifier + noun.
segments.s167-g280: کزو = "from whom / from which" (adverbial/relative particle) attaches to the question. شهریارا is a vocative address "O King." چه دیدی گناه = interrogative structure: چه = "what/which," دیدی = past 2nd-person "did you see," گناه = "fault/sin" (object). The phrase asks "O King, from whom did you see any fault?"
segments.s167-g193: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when.' The clause is arranged subject (سیاوش) + temporal marker (چو) + prepositional phrase (با جفت غمها) + verb (بگفت). بگفت is an archaic/poetic past form (بَ‑ + گفت) meaning 'he spoke/said.' جفت غمها is a genitival noun phrase 'pair of griefs' (جفت = 'pair', غمها = 'griefs').
segments.s167-g194: This clause presents the action: adjective/participle خروشان ('roaring, tumultuous') modifies the following verb phrase. بدو is the dative clitic 'to him/unto him.' اندر آویخت is a past transitive verb phrase 'clung to / hung on,' with جفت (the subject 'the pair') placed at the end for emphasis — a typical poetic inversion.
segments.s167-g195: رخش (the horse's name or 'steed') is the subject. پر ز + noun is a poetic way to say 'full of' (پر = 'full (of)', ز = 'of'). دل و دیده are coordinated nouns ('heart and eye') and گشت is the past of گشتن 'became/turned' applying to both nouns; the verb follows the coordinated objects, typical in Persian word order.
segments.s167-g196: سوی is the preposition 'toward,' followed by the noun آخر 'end/rear.' تازی here qualifies اسپان: تازی اسپان = 'Tazi horses' (i.e. Arabian horses). گذشت is the past of گذشتن 'passed.' The clause literal order is 'toward end Tazi horses passed' with the subject supplied from previous clause (the steed/actor).
segments.s167-g197: بیاورد is the narrative/past form 'brought' (بَ‑ + آورد). شبرنگ is the direct object (a variegated item or name) and بهزاد را is the beneficiary/goal with the definite-object marker را; classical Persian allows flexibility in word order, so 'بیاورد شبرنگ بهزاد را' = 'he brought the shabrang to Behzad.'
segments.s167-g198: که introduces a subordinate or reported clause 'that / saying that.' دریافتی here has an optative/hortative flavor in the narrative — 'may you receive' or 'you shall receive' (classical usage can carry a purposive/wish sense). روز کین is 'the day of vengeance,' and باد after the noun is the optative particle 'may' (a fossilized wish/optative). را here is the object marker attached to باد phrase as final emphasis.
segments.s167-g199: خروشان ('roaring') again functions as a manner/participial modifier. سرش را is the object 'his head' (سر + possessive clitic ش + object marker را). به بر در is a compact classical prepositional phrase meaning roughly 'to/in his embrace' or 'into his chest/arms' (به + بر + در), and گرفت is the simple past 'seized/took.'
segments.s167-g200: لگام و فسارش are two coordinated nouns for bridle/reins ('lugaam' and 'fasaar' both meaning restraint/bridle). ز + سر + برگرفت is a verbal construction meaning 'took off/from the head' (برگرفت = 'took away/removed,' ز سر = 'from the head'). The subject is the same agent as the previous clause.
segments.s167-g121: دو بهره چو از تیره شب در گذشت is a temporal clause: 'دو بهره' (lit. 'two portions') here means 'two watches/parts (of the night)'; چو introduces a temporal 'when' clause; word order is Subject (دو بهره) + temporal marker چو + prepositional phrase از تیره شب (from the dark night) + verbal predicate درگذشت (passed). گذشت is a past-tense perfective verb meaning 'passed'.
segments.s167-g122: طلایه هم آنگه بیامد ز دشت is the main clause: طلایه (the vanguard) is subject; هم 'also/likewise' gives a linking/emphatic sense; آنگه 'then/thereupon' specifies immediacy; بیامد is past tense 'came'; ز دشت is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from the plain'. Word order is Subject + particles/adverbials + verb + origin phrase.
segments.s167-g123: که here begins a dependent/explanatory clause ('for'/'that'); افراسیاب is the proper-name subject; و links the name to the following noun; فراوان modifies سپاه ('a plentiful/great army'). The phrase functions as the subject of the following verb. Word order is conjunction + name + coordinator + adjective + noun.
segments.s167-g124: پدید آمد is the verbal core meaning 'appeared/emerged' (past tense); از دور is a prepositional phrase 'from afar'; تازان is a participial/adjectival plural meaning 'rushing/charging ones' that characterizes the army's movement; به راه is a directional phrase 'onto/toward the road'. Word order places the verb before the origin and manner/location phrases, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s167-g125: ز نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'from near(by)'; گرسیوز is the proper name (Garsivaz) functioning as possessor or source; آمد is the past-tense verb 'came'; نوند here is a following name/word (likely a proper name or exclamatory element) that is syntactically the subject or vocative following the verb—poetic word order permits this inversion (verb then subject).
segments.s167-g126: که introduces reported speech or a subordinate clause ('that'/'saying that'); بر is a preposition 'on/upon' or directional 'toward'; چاره جان is a noun phrase 'the remedy/means of life' (چاره modifying جان); میان را uses میان 'middle/mid-way' with the object marker را (marking میان as definite/direct object); ببند is the present imperative 'close/shut' addressed to the listeners. The clause therefore issues an imperative reported by the preceding verb.
segments.s167-g127: نیامد is a negative past ('did not come'/'did not result'); ز گفتار من is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from my speech' (i.e., 'by my words'); هیچ سود is an object phrase 'any/no benefit' with هیچ emphasizing negation. The whole clause states that no benefit resulted from the speaker's words.
segments.s167-g128: از آتش ندیدم is literally 'from the fire I did not see' (ندیدم = I did not see); جز introduces an exception 'except'; از تیره دود ('from/of dark smoke') states what was seen — here only dark smoke. Word order is ablative phrase + negative verb + exception phrase; ندیدم is first-person past negative.
segments.s167-g409: درختی is an indefinite noun 'a tree' (the -ی here makes it 'a certain/one tree'); نشانی is a noun 'sign' placed after it in apposition; همی is the archaic/poetic continuous particle ('was/stood'); بر زمین is a prepositional phrase 'upon the ground' (بر + direct noun). The word order (noun — appositive — aspect particle — prepositional phrase) is typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s167-g410: کجا is a relative/adverb 'where'; برگ 'leaf' is the subject of آورد 'brought/produced' (آورد here = brought/about); خون 'blood' is the direct object of آورد; بار کین is a nominal phrase 'the load/burden of vengeance' (بار = burden/weight, modified by the genitive کین). The clause describes the tree's portent: a leaf that 'brought blood' and thus is the 'burden of vengeance.'
segments.s167-g411: به کین is a prepositional phrase 'out of/for vengeance'; سیاوش is the subject; سیه پوشد is a descriptive verbal phrase 'he put on black (garments)' where سیه is the adjective 'black' and پوشد (classical narrative form) = 'he put on/was clothed in.' آب appears here as a standalone noun 'water' (likely figurative, tied to the following clause or indicating tears/ritual water); its placement is poetic and may link to the next verb.
segments.s167-g412: کند is the verbal root 'does/makes' (narrative use here = 'he made/uttered'); زار is an adjective/adverb 'wretched/pitiful' modifying نفرین 'curse'; نفرین is the noun 'curse'; به + افراسیاب marks the target 'against Afrasiab.' Together: 'he made a pitiful/wretched curse upon Afrasiab.'
segments.s167-g413: ستمگاره‌ای is an indefinite agent noun 'an oppressor' or 'one who commits injustice' (the -ای gives a sense of 'a certain person who...'); بر تن خویشتن is a reflexive prepositional phrase 'upon his own body' (بر = on; تن = body; خویشتن = oneself/one's own), so the whole phrase labels someone who oppresses his own person.
segments.s167-g414: بسی is an adverb 'much' or 'greatly'; یادت آید is an idiom where یادت ('your memory' or 'you remember') + آید ('comes') = 'you will remember' or 'it will occur to you'; ز گفتار من is a prepositional phrase 'from my words' (ز = from; گفتار = words/speech; من = my/I). The phrase means 'you will recall much from my speech.'
segments.s167-g415: نه اندر شکاری is a negated locative phrase 'not in a hunt'; که introduces a relative clause 'which/where'; گور is the animal 'onager' here (not 'grave'); افگنی uses the poetic/derivative form of افگن (here functioning as a verbalizing/causative element) meaning 'to cause to fall/strike down' or more generally 'to affect' — so 'which strikes down the onager' (the exact nuance is poetic).
segments.s167-g416: دگر آهوان را is the object phrase 'the other/dear gazelles' (دگر = other; آهوان = deer/gazelles; را = definite object marker). به شور افگنی uses the same verbalizing/causative افگنی ('to cause') with شور 'tumult/commotion' introduced by به, so the meaning is 'you cause the other gazelles into tumult' — i.e., you scatter them or set them running in agitation.
segments.s167-g97: بپرسید is a past/pluperfect-style narrative form meaning 'they asked' (third-person plural); ز + و here functions as a classical compound for 'از او' meaning 'from her' or 'of her' and introduces the addressee of the question; دخت افراسیاب is a genitival phrase 'the daughter of Afrasiab' and is the person being asked. Word order is flexible/classical: verb first, then a prepositional/oblique phrase marking from whom the question is directed, then the noun phrase identifying that person.
segments.s167-g98: که introduces the reported question ('that' / 'saying'); فرزانه is an epithetical adjective 'wise' placed before the vocative شاها ('O king!') — شاها is a direct address particle; چه دیدی is a past second-person interrogative 'what did you see' (دیدی = 'you saw'); به خواب is a prepositional phrase 'in sleep/in a dream' modifying دیدی. The whole segment is the reported speech clause introduced by که.
segments.s167-g99: سیاوش is the speaker's name; بدو is the clitic/prepositional phrase 'to him/to her' (بدو = به + او) indicating the addressee of گفت; گفت is the simple past 'he said'; کز here functions as a classical preposition/relative 'from/because of' and begins the content 'from my dream' (خواب من). The clause has verb گفت after the indirect object clitic, a common word order in narrative Persian.
segments.s167-g100: لبت 'your lip(s)' (literally) stands for speech; هیچ before an imperative intensifies the negation ('not at all' / 'do not ever'); مگشای is the negative imperative formed with the prefix م- plus the second-person singular present-stem (گشای ~ open), meaning 'do not open' or 'do not utter'; بر انجمن is a locative phrase 'before/at the assembly' specifying where the speech should not be revealed. The sequence is a prohibitive instruction directed to the addressee.
segments.s167-g101: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the perception verb; دیدم is the first-person past 'I saw'; ای is the vocative particle 'O' used to address someone; سرو سیمین is a compound epithet 'silver cypress' (a poetic epithet for a beautiful/tall person). به خواب is the adverbial phrase 'in a dream' and attaches to دیدم. Word order places the manner/adverbial and vocative around the verb in typical epic diction.
segments.s167-g102: که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that ...'; بودی is the second-person past form 'you were'; یکی functions as an indefinite article 'a/one' before a predicate noun; بی‌کران is an adjective 'boundless/endless' modifying رود; رود آب is a noun phrase 'river of water' (رود = river, آب = water) — the predicate describes the addressee in the dream. The clause is a reported vision introduced by که.
segments.s167-g103: یکی begins the contrastive description 'on one (side)' or 'one was ...'; کوه آتش is a genitive phrase 'a mountain of fire' (noun + modifier); به دیگر کران is a locative/contrastive phrase 'toward the other edge/bank' (به = to/toward, دیگر کران = the other shore/edge). The line juxtaposes two opposing banks or sides with different images.
segments.s167-g104: گرفته is a participial/adjectival form 'seized/held' or 'occupied' describing position; لب آب 'the lip/brink of the water' is the place phrase (لب = brink, آب = water); نیزه وران is a compound meaning 'spear‑bearers/spearmen' (نیزه = spear, -وران a plural/agentive element forming 'those possessing wielding spears'). The segment describes spearmen occupying the water's edge; word order places the participle before the location for vivid epic effect.
segments.s167-g257: This segment contains a fronted object phrase plus the conditional introducer. مرا (me+object marker) is placed before the rest for emphasis; چرخ گردان is a nominative phrase 'the turning wheel' (i.e. fate) that functions as the subject of the conditional clause; اگر opens the 'if' clause and بی‌گناه is an adjectival phrase modifying مرا ('me, innocent'). Word order is emphatic/poetic: object + subject + if + modifier.
segments.s167-g258: به دست بدان is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into the hand(s) of those (people)' where ب + دان here functions as an older/prosaic form 'to those'; کرد is the causative/past stem 'made/put' and خواهد is a future auxiliary giving the clause its future/hypothetical force; تباه is a predicate adjective 'ruined/destroyed'. Grammatically this is: prepositional phrase (destination) + light verb (کرد) + future auxiliary (خواهد) + predicate (تباه).
segments.s167-g259: به مردی here functions like 'for a man' or 'in a man'; کنون is the adverb 'now'. زور و آهنگ نیست is a coordinated predicate 'there is no strength and no intent' with the negative copula نیست at the end. Persian places the negated predicate after the subject/phrase, so the clause reads 'as for a man now, strength and intent do not exist.'
segments.s167-g260: که introduces a relative clause modifying مردی ('who/that'), با + کردگار جهان marks the opponent 'with the Creator of the world', and جنگ نیست is another negative existential: 'there is no fight (with)'. Together it means 'who does not/would not fight with the Creator (i.e. has no strength to contend with God)'; word order is subject/phrase then relative clause ending with the negative copula نیست.
segments.s167-g261: سرآمد is a noun meaning 'foremost/chief' used as the head of the clause. بریشان here functions as an older/prepositional phrase ('bar‑īshān' = 'over/among them'), so the phrase together marks 'the foremost of them'. بر آن روزگار is a time phrase: بر = 'in/on', آن = 'that', روزگار = 'time/age' — 'at that time' (poetic word order: the time phrase follows the noun).
segments.s167-g262: همه is the plural subject 'all/everyone'. کشته گشتند is a periphrastic passive: کشته (past participle 'killed') + گشتند (past of گشتن 'became/turned') = 'were killed'. و links the clauses. برگشته کار is a poetic inversion (participle before noun): برگشته = 'turned back/returned' modifying کار = 'the affair/matter' — i.e. 'and the affair was reversed/turned back' (copula is omitted as is common in verse).
segments.s167-g263: ز تیر و ز ژوپین is a double prepositional phrase 'from arrows and from the gambeson' (ز = 'from/because of'). ببد is an archaic past copula/verb (bud → 'was/became'). خسته is a predicative adjective 'weary/tired'. شاه is the subject placed after the predicate for poetic emphasis: 'the king was worn out because of arrows and the coat.'
segments.s167-g264: نگون is an adjective meaning 'overthrown/ill‑fated' placed before the verb for emphasis. اندر آمد is a compound verb 'came in/entered' (اندر = 'in/into', آمد = past 'came'). ز پشت سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'from behind the army' (ز = 'from', پشت = 'rear/behind', سپاه = 'host/army'). Together: 'overthrown, he came down/from the rear of the host.'
segments.s167-g145: گر is the conditional particle 'if' introducing the protasis. ایوان من is a possessive phrase 'my iwan/porch' (word order: noun + possessor). سر به کیوان کشید is a verbal phrase: سر 'head/top' + به 'towards' + کیوان 'Kayvân/Saturn/sky' + کشید, here a past/simple perfect verb meaning 'stretched/raised' — together 'raised its head toward Kayvân'. The whole segment is a conditional clause ('If my iwan raised its head to Kayvân...').
segments.s167-g146: همان 'that same' picks up the result; زهر گیتی is a genitive construction 'the world's poison' (زهر 'poison', گیتی 'world'). بباید is a modal/impersonal construction 'one must / it is necessary' (present/obligative), followed by چشید 'to taste/experience' used as the verb required by بباید — together meaning 'one must taste/experience'. The order (بباید + infinitive/past-form) is an older Persian idiom for necessity.
segments.s167-g147: اگر 'if' introduces another conditional; سال گردد is 'if the year becomes' (گردد is the subjunctive/optative/present of گشت 'to become/turn'). هزار و دویست uses و as the connective 'and' between the numerals to mean 'one thousand and two hundred' (i.e. 1,200). The whole is 'if the year turns/comes to 1200.'
segments.s167-g148: بجز 'except' begins the exception; خاک تیره 'dark earth' (خاک here is the grave/dust/earth and تیره 'dark/bleak' qualifies it). مرا جای نیست is an impersonal existential construction meaning 'there is no place for me' (مرا = 'for me' as object/benefactive, جای = 'place', نیست = 'does not exist/is not'). Word order places the exception first, then the clause stating lack of any other place.
segments.s167-g149: ز 'from' (poetic preposition) + شب 'night' + روشنایی 'brightness/light' form 'from the night, light' (i.e. 'light from night/darkness'). نجوید is a negative or non-assertive present form of جستن 'to seek' placed before کسی; classical Persian often uses this inverted order to mean 'no one seeks'. Thus the clause reads 'No one seeks light from the night.'
segments.s167-g150: کجا 'where' opens a rhetorical question. بهره دارد is 'has benefit' (بهره 'benefit/share', دارد 'has'). ز دانش بسی uses ز 'from' + دانش 'knowledge' and بسی 'much/enough' to mean 'much benefit from knowledge' — the whole clause asks rhetorically 'Where is there much benefit from knowledge?' (i.e. 'what use is much learning?').
segments.s167-g151: ترا (here written as ت + را) is the dative/oblique 'to you/for you' (ت is the 2nd-person clitic part and را is the object marker fused in speech/writing). پنج ماهست is a poetic contraction meaning 'you are five months' (پنج ماه + است → پنج ماه است), with ماهست written together; the clause ends with ز آبستنی 'from pregnancy' indicating 'you are five months from pregnancy' or 'your pregnancy is five months long.'
segments.s167-g152: ازین 'from this' (از + این, here written از + یِن) introduces the source; نامور‌گر is a compressed conditional/qualifying expression where نامور 'famous' combines with the conditional particle گر 'if' (so 'if [it] were famous' or 'should it be famed'). بود is the past/conditional of بودن 'to be' and رستنی here denotes 'offspring/what is produced' (an old noun meaning produce/born thing). Together the segment expresses a conditional: 'If from this a famed offspring were to be.'
segments.s167-g57: به سوی سیاووش بنهاد روی is a fronted prepositional phrase followed by the verb+object. به سوی (preposition + noun) = 'toward' and is placed before the verb for emphasis; بنهاد is the past tense (3rd sing.) of بنهادن 'to set/put' used here as 'turned/placed', and روی (face) is the object of that verb (i.e. 'set/turned his face').
segments.s167-g58: ابا is an archaic/poetic verb meaning 'to refuse' or 'be reluctant' (here 3rd sing. past 'he refused'); it takes the following noun phrase نامداران پرخاشجوی as its object. نامداران is the plural noun 'renowned ones' modified by the adjective پرخاشجوی 'quarrelsome/aggressive'. Word order is verb then object, which is common in older Persian poetry.
segments.s167-g59: بدانگه که is a temporal/conjunctive phrase: بدانگه 'thereupon/at that moment' + که 'when/that' introduces the clause. گرسیوز بدفریب is a name + epithet: the proper name followed by the adjective بدفریب 'treacherous/deceitful', i.e. 'Garsivaz the treacherous.'
segments.s167-g60: گران کرد is the adjectival root + کردن pattern meaning 'to make heavy' (گران 'heavy' + کرد 'made'). بر زین is the locative/prepositional phrase 'on/upon the saddle' (بر = on, زین = saddle). دوال رکیب is a compound/attributive phrase 'doval (twofold/pair) + rākib (rider/companion)' i.e. 'for the pair of riders' (classical Persian often omits an explicit ezāfe).
segments.s167-g61: سیاوش (subject) + به پرده درآمد is a motion/entry construction: به پرده 'into/behind the curtain' (directional) + درآمد (past tense 'entered/came in'). به درد here expresses his condition 'in pain' or 'with pain' — a prepositional phrase indicating his state as he enters.
segments.s167-g62: به تن لرز لرزان is a compact descriptive phrase: به تن 'on/over the body' (indicating the experiencer), followed by the noun لرز 'tremor' and the adjective لرزان 'trembling' (repetition intensifies the state). و رخساره زرد links with a coordinating conjunction 'and' to give his facial appearance: رخساره (face/complexion) + زرد (pale/wan). Word order is noun followed by adjective (typical in Persian).
segments.s167-g63: فرنگیس گفت is subject + past verb 'Farangis said.' ای is a vocative particle 'O!', and گو here functions as an imperative/intonational particle meaning 'say' or 'speak' used to call or address someone; شیرچنگ is a vocative epithet ('lion-claw') addressed to the addressee.
segments.s167-g64: چه بودت که is an idiomatic classical question: چه بودت (literally 'what was to you') = 'what happened to you' or 'what is the matter with you', followed by که introducing the result/secondary clause. دیگر شدستی به رنگ contains شدستی, an archaic 2nd-person past form meaning 'you became' (شدن 'to become' + archaic ending), and به رنگ 'in color/complexion' — together 'you have become of a different color/changed in complexion.'
segments.s167-g41: This is a list of two nominal phrases joined by و (and). Each phrase is a noun سپاهی 'an army/force' followed by the poetic preposition ز 'from' and the place name (روم, چین). Word order is noun + 'from' + place (rather than 'from X an army'), which is common in classical Persian.
segments.s167-g42: همی is an archaic/classical particle marking habitual or continuous aspect ('would/ever'), هر زمان = 'every/whenever', and the verb برخروشد is a third-person verbal form meaning 'arise/occur' placed before its oblique complement زمین 'land/realm'. The phrase therefore says that such armies repeatedly/at any time appear on the land.
segments.s167-g43: تو is the subject 'you'. در کار او is a prepositional phrase 'in his business/affairs'. او‌گر is the archaic conditional 'if' (a fused form of اگر). درنگ آوری is a light-verb construction: درنگ 'delay' + آوری (2nd person) literally 'you bring delay' i.e. 'you hesitate / you tarry'.
segments.s167-g44: مگر here introduces a mitigated/hesitative sense ('perhaps' or 'it may be'), followed by باد, the optative particle meaning 'may' or 'let it be so'. زان پس = 'after that/thereafter'. به چنگ آوری is an idiom: به + چنگ + آوری literally 'bring into the claw' → 'seize/capture' (second person).
segments.s167-g45: و‌گر is the conditional 'and if'. دیر گیری تو is an idiom: دیر 'late' + گیری (second-person verb form of گرفتن) = 'you are slow / you delay' (literally 'you take long'). جنگ آورد puts جنگ 'war' with the verb آورد 'brings/causes' to indicate that war will bring the following result.
segments.s167-g46: دو کشور = 'two countries/realms'. به مردی = 'to/into a man' (i.e. into the possession of one man). به چنگ آورد is the result clause: به چنگ (into the claw/hand) + آورد 'brings' = 'brings into the grasp/seizes'. Word order places objects and indirect phrases before the final verb, as is typical.
segments.s167-g47: و‌گر = 'and if'. سوی ایران = 'toward Iran' (سوی = toward). براند is a verbal form (from راندن) meaning 'drives/advances' (3rd person), and سپاه 'army' is the subject; classical word order allows the verb before the subject or with subject after it.
segments.s167-g48: که here introduces a subordinate/result clause 'that/so that' or a descriptive relative 'who'. یارد شدن is a compound meaning 'to become a companion/ally' (یارد = companion + شدن 'to become'). پیش او = 'before him / in his presence', and کینه‌خواه is a predicative noun 'avenger' or 'one who seeks vengeance' — the clause says that such a companion becomes an avenger in front of him.
segments.s167-g241: چو is the temporal subordinating particle 'when' introducing the clause; گفتار گرسیوز is a genitive phrase 'the words/speech of Garsivaz' (noun followed by possessor), and افراسیاب is the subject (proper name) placed before the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s167-g242: شنید is the simple past 'he heard' with its subject (افراسیاب) carried over from the previous segment; و is the coordinating 'and' linking clauses; برآمد is an intransitive past 'arose/issued forth'; بلند modifies آفتاب ('high' or 'lofty') and appears before the noun in a poetic inversion, so برآمد بلند آفتاب = 'the sun rose high'.
segments.s167-g243: به ترکان is the indirect object 'to the Turks' placed before the verb; بفرمود is the honorific/past verb 'he commanded/ordered'; کاندر introduces the content clause meaning 'that/therein' (classical contraction), and دهید is the present-subjunctive/imperative 'give/put' expressing the commanded action.
segments.s167-g244: درین is the poetic contraction of در + این 'in this'; دشت is 'plain' (location). کشتی is 'ship(s)' here as the object of the command; به marks accompaniment/instrument ('with' or 'by') before خون 'blood'; برنهید is the subjunctive/imperative of برنهادن 'place/raise/put upon', completing the commanded phrase 'set ships with blood'.
segments.s167-g245: از ایران is the locative/source phrase 'from Iran'; سپه بود is an inverted copular phrase 'there was an army' with بود (past 'was'); مردی هزار is a classical numeric expression — مردی here functions as part of the count 'a thousand men' (poetic ordering: noun + numeral).
segments.s167-g246: همه نامدار means 'all renowned' (quantifier + adjective); از در is a poetic prepositional cluster meaning 'in/on the' or 'in the midst of', and کارزار is 'battle' or 'field of war', so the whole phrase qualifies the army as famed in battle.
segments.s167-g247: رده بر کشیدند is a verbal phrase 'they drew up (their) ranks' where رده is the object 'rank(s)', بر is a verbal prefix/particle forming the compound action, and کشیدند is the past tense 'they drew'; ایرانیان is the subject, placed after the verb by poetic inversion.
segments.s167-g248: ببستند is past 'they closed/they made fast' (here 'they closed in/form up'); خون ریختن is a verbal noun 'the shedding/pouring of blood' functioning as the object and is marked by را (definite object marker), and میان 'between' indicates the locus — together: 'they closed in and the shedding of blood occurred between (the forces)'.
segments.s167-g353: سپاهی (the army/the troops) is the (definite) object/topic placed before the verb. بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning “in this manner” (بدین + گونه). کردی is the 2nd-person singular past of کردن, here functioning as the main verb “you did/made,” and تباه is a predicate adjective/complement (“ruined”), so کردی تباه = “you ruined.” Classical Persian often omits the object marker را after such direct objects.
segments.s167-g354: نگر is the 2nd-person imperative ‘look!/observe!’. تا introduces the subordinate/result clause here (‘so that/that’). چگونه is the interrogative adverb ‘how’, بود is the past copula ‘was’, and رای شاه is the noun phrase ‘the king’s counsel/decision’ (رای = counsel/plan, modified by شاه). The clause means “see how the king’s counsel was.”
segments.s167-g355: اگر is the conditional ‘if’. خود is an emphatic/reflexive pronoun ‘yourself/you yourself’. نیازردتی is the negative past-form (2nd sing.) ‘you did not hurt / you had not hurt’ (classical negative prefix نی-). از نخست is an adverbial phrase ‘from the first / at the beginning’ placed after the verb.
segments.s167-g356: به آب is a prepositional phrase ‘in/with/to the water’ (instrumental/medium). این گنه را is the definite object ‘this sin’ marked by را. توانست is the past of توانستن ‘was able/could’, and شست is the past of شستن ‘wash’; in classical Persian the sequence توانست + past-verb expresses ability/possibility (‘could wash’). The subject is omitted (understood from context).
segments.s167-g357: کنون = ‘now’. آن (literally ‘that/the thing’) functions as a dummy or demonstrative subject ‘that which/what’. به آید is a classical periphrasis meaning ‘will come/happen’ (به + آید). که introduces the following subordinate clause, and اندر جهان is the locative phrase ‘in the world’ (اندر = in). Together they form the phrase ‘Now that which will come to pass, that in the world…’.
segments.s167-g358: نباشد is the negative present/jussive form of بودن here with a future/result sense (‘there will not be / will not remain’). پدید (پدید آمدن) means ‘appear/come to light’ or more generally ‘exist manifestly’. آشکار و نهان are coordinated adjectives ‘apparent (manifest) and hidden’ (i.e. nothing either manifest or concealed will be found). The verb negation precedes the predicate; there is no overt copula.
segments.s167-g359: بدیشان is the classical contraction of به ایشان ‘to them’ (indirect object) and is fronted. چنین modifies پاسخ (‘suchly/thus an answer’). پاسخ آورد is a common classical collocation ‘brought an answer’ = ‘answered’, with آورد (past of آوردن) as the main verb. شاه at the end names the agent ‘the king’ (subject placed after the verb is common in classical Persian).
segments.s167-g360: کزو is a relative pronoun ‘from which/from whom’. من is the subject ‘I’. ندیدم is the 1st-person past negative of دیدن ‘I did not see’. به دیده is an instrumental/locative phrase ‘in (my) sight / with (my) eye’ (به + دیده). گناه is the object ‘sin’. Word order follows classical patterns: relative + subject + verb + instrumental phrase + object.
segments.s167-g177: وز ایران بیاید یکی چاره‌گر — 'وز' is an archaic/poetic form equivalent to 'از' (from) and begins a prepositional phrase 'from Iran'. 'بیاید' is a 3rd-person present/future-form (classical narrative) meaning 'will come'. 'یکی' before the noun functions as an indefinite determiner 'a certain', and 'چاره‌گر' is a noun 'solver/deliverer' that completes the subject.
segments.s167-g178: به فرمان دادار بسته کمر — 'به فرمان دادار' is a prepositional agent phrase 'by/at the command of the Creator' (دادار = Creator). 'بسته' is the past participle of بستن used predicatively ('having bound' / 'girded'), and 'کمر' is the object 'waist/loins' (کمر بستن = to gird one's loins). The phrase describes how the deliverer comes（i.e. girded by God's command).
segments.s167-g179: از ایدر ترا با پسر ناگهان — 'از ایدر' = 'from here' (ایدر = here). 'ت' is the 2nd-person object suffix and 'را' is the definite/object marker (together ترا = 'you' as direct object). 'با پسر' = 'with (a/the) son'. 'ناگهان' is the adverb 'suddenly' placed before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s167-g180: سوی رود جیحون برد در نهان — 'سوی رود جیحون' = 'toward the Jeyhun river' (سوی = toward, رود جیحون = the river Jeyhun). 'برد' here is a narrative verb meaning 'they carry/take' (contextual sense: carry/take away), and 'در نهان' = 'in secret/secretly'. Word order places the direction phrase before the verb, as is common in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s167-g181: نشانند بر تخت شاهی ورا — 'نشانند' (from نشاندن) is a 3rd-person plural narrative verb 'they seat/they set'. 'بر تخت شاهی' = 'on the royal throne' (بر = on, تخت = throne, شاهی = royal/kingship). 'ورا' = 'to him' (i.e., they seat him on the throne).
segments.s167-g182: به فرمان بود مرغ و ماهی ورا — literally 'by command were bird and fish to him'; 'به فرمان' = 'by command/at the command', 'بود' is the copula 'were/was' (past/narrative), and 'مرغ و ماهی ورا' is the nominal phrase 'bird and fish (to him)'. The construction means the bird and fish were at his command or subject to him.
segments.s167-g183: ز گیتی برآرد سراسر خروش — 'ز گیتی' = 'from the world' (ز = from, poetic). 'برآرد' is a classical 3rd-person verb 'brings forth/raises'. 'سراسر خروش' = 'an uproar throughout' (سراسر = throughout, خروش = roar/uproar). Together: 'he raises an uproar throughout the world.'
segments.s167-g184: زمانه ز کیخسرو آید به جوش — 'زمانه' = 'the age/the times'. 'ز کیخسرو' uses ز as a causal 'because of Kay Khosrow'. 'آید' is the 3rd-person present/future 'will come', and 'به جوش' is an idiom 'to a boil/into turmoil' (جوش = boiling/surge). So: 'the age, because of Kay Khosrow, will come to a boil.'
segments.s167-g313: This noun phrase strings together kin/titles: پدر (father) + شاه (the king) joined by و (and) to رستمش (Rostam with the possessive -ش, 'his Rostam' — i.e. Rostam belonging/related to that king). پروردگار (Lord) follows as a predicate or vocative, so the whole can be read as addressing or describing a single referent: 'the Lord, father of the king and of his Rostam.' Note the possessive suffix -ش attaches to رستم to mark 'his Rostam' and و simply coordinates the two nouns.
segments.s167-g314: بپیچی is an optative/jussive form (prefix ب- + پیچی from پیچیدن) expressing a wish or command sense here: 'may (you/he) turn' or 'make to turn.' The preposition به introduces the goal 'to' or 'toward' and فرجام زین روزگار is a prepositional noun phrase 'the end/outcome of these times' — note زین is an archaic/poetic demonstrative 'this' (so 'these times'), and روزگار = 'time/age.' Together the phrase means 'may (it/he) be turned to the end of this age.'
segments.s167-g315: چو / چون here function as the subordinating particle 'when' or 'as' introducing a temporal clause, followed by a list of proper names joined by و ('and'). The repetition of چو/چون is a common poetic device meaning 'as/like' before each name; word order is simply the coordinated list of actors.
segments.s167-g316: ببندند is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form (ب- prefix + بندند) used in narrative after چو to mean 'they bind/fasten.' بر introduces the location/target 'upon' and the sequence کوهه پیل کوس groups locative + modifier + object: کوهه (mountain) modifies پیل (elephant) and کوس (war-drum) is the thing being fastened; the phrase means 'they fasten the drum upon the elephant on the mountain.'
segments.s167-g317: This is an appositive phrase: دمنده ('the blower' = trumpeter) followed by سپهبد ('the general, spahbed') and گو functioning as an attributive 'called/named' (classical usage, '-گو' = 'so-called') attaching to پیلتن (a place/name). Read as 'the trumpeter, the spahbed called Piltan.'
segments.s167-g318: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause 'for/that' here. خوارند is a predicate meaning 'are despised/held low' (3rd-person plural). بر چشم او is a prepositional phrase 'in/at his eye' = 'in his sight,' and انجمن ('assembly') is the noun affected; the structure places the predicate before the noun (permissible in classical Persian), yielding 'for in his eyes the assembly is despised.'
segments.s167-g319: This nominative phrase names the warrior with epithets: فریبرز (personal name) followed by کاووس (Kavus, here in possessive/appositive relation as Kavus's) and the descriptive درنده شیر ('ferocious lion') — شیر is metaphorical for a brave warrior and درنده qualifies it as 'ferocious.' The order places name, patron (Kavus), then descriptive epithet.
segments.s167-g320: که begins a relative clause 'who/whom.' هرگز (never) negates ندیدش (ن + دید + ش = 'saw him' with ش = object 'him'); کس ('anyone') follows the verb as the implicit subject 'no one.' از جنگ سیر is a prepositional complement 'sated from/by war' (سیر = satiety). Together: 'whom no one ever saw sated by battle.'
segments.s167-g113: This is a short reporting clause: the name 'فرنگیس' (Farangis) is the subject, followed by the narrative past verb 'گفت' (said). 'این به جز نیکوی' is the reported content introduced without a quote marker; 'این' (= 'this') is the demonstrative/topic and 'به جز' is the idiom 'except' with 'نیکوی' (= 'goodness') as the excluded item. Word order is Subject - Verb - (reported clause fragment).
segments.s167-g114: This clause completes Farangis's speech. 'نباشد' is a negative verbal form meaning 'is not / there is no', used here in a classical/poetic way (not the modern imperative). 'نگر' is an imperative 'look' or 'consider'; 'یک زمان' = 'for a time / a little while'; 'بغنوی' (from غنود) means 'to fall asleep / to slumber'. The sequence gives an exhortation after the statement: 'there is nothing but goodness — look, sleep for a while.'
segments.s167-g115: 'به گرسیوز آید همی بخت شوم' is a compact, archaic-looking clause meaning 'an unlucky fate may come upon Garsivaz.' 'به' marks direction/toward, 'گرسیوز' is the name, 'آید' is the verb 'comes/happens', and the particle 'همی' is an older emphatic/continuous/optative particle here colouring the verb as potential/inevitable. 'بخت' = 'fate/fortune' and 'شوم' is the negative/ill-omened sense 'ill‑fated' (here describing the fortune).
segments.s167-g116: 'شود کشته بر دست سالار روم' is a passive/result clause: 'شود' (becomes/happens) + 'کشته' (killed) yields 'he will be killed.' 'بر دست' = 'by the hand of' (agent phrase), where 'دست' is literal 'hand' used idiomatically for 'by the power of', and 'سالار روم' names the agent 'the commander (سالار) of Rome/Romans.' Word order places the verbal phrase first then the agent phrase.
segments.s167-g117: This is a simple past narrative clause. 'سیاوش' is the subject; 'سپه را' is the definite direct object marked by 'را' ('the army'); 'سراسر' is an adverb/adjective 'throughout/entire'; and 'بخواند' here is the narrative past meaning 'called/summoned' (classical poetic past form with the prefix b-). Word order is Subject — Object (with را) — Adverb — Verb.
segments.s167-g118: This clause states where he stayed. 'به' = 'to/at', 'درگاه ایوان' = 'the court/portico of the iwan' (درگاه used as 'royal court/threshold' and ایوان the architectural iwan), 'زمانی' = 'for a time / for a while', and 'بماند' is the past narrative 'remained' (classical form). The order is locative phrase + temporal adverb + verb.
segments.s167-g119: 'بسیچید و بنشست' is two coordinated past verbs: 'bسیچید' (he mustered/assembled [the troops]) and 'بنشست' (he sat down). 'خنجر به چنگ' is an instrumental phrase 'dagger in hand' (خنجر = dagger, 'به چنگ' = in the grip/hand). The order Verb — Verb — instrumental phrase is typical narrative sequence.
segments.s167-g120: 'طلایه فرستاد بر سوی گنگ' is another past-action clause: 'طلایه' = 'vanguard' is the direct theme, 'فرستاد' = 'sent/dispatched' (past), 'بر سوی' = 'toward' (prepositional direction), and 'گنگ' is here the destination/name. The verb is transitive and comes after the noun 'طلایه' for emphasis.
segments.s167-g1: دبیر پژوهنده را پیش خواند is a verb phrase with object before the verb: 'دبیر پژوهنده' (a compound noun: the inquisitive/attentive scribe) is marked by the object marker 'را', and 'پیش خواند' is a compound verbal idea meaning 'called/summoned forward'. The past tense verb 'خواند' here has the sense 'to call/summon', not 'to read'.
segments.s167-g2: سخنهای آگنده را برفشاند places the (plural) object 'سخنهای آگنده' before the verb; 'آگنده' qualifies 'سخنها' as 'laden/weighty/abundant', and the past verb 'برفشاند' (lit. 'scattered') is used metaphorically as 'poured forth' or 'dispersed' speeches/words.
segments.s167-g3: نخست آفریننده را یاد کرد: 'نخست' is an adverb ('first/at first') placed before the clause; 'آفریننده' is the direct object marked by 'را'; 'یاد کرد' is a common Persian periphrasis meaning 'remembered' (literally 'made remembrance').
segments.s167-g4: ز وام خرد جانش آزاد کرد = 'he freed his life from the debt of wisdom': 'ز' (poetic 'از') means 'from', 'وام خرد' is a genitive-like phrase 'the debt/obligation of wisdom', 'جانش' is 'his life/soul' (possessive -ش), and 'آزاد کرد' is the past-tense transitive 'freed' with 'جانش' as its object.
segments.s167-g5: ازان پس is a fixed adverbial phrase 'after that/thereafter'; 'خرد را ستایش گرفت' literally 'took/received praise for wisdom' uses 'گرفت' (took/received) with 'ستایش' as the action received toward the object 'خرد' (marked by 'را'). This is an idiomatic passive-like construction formed with گرفت.
segments.s167-g6: ابر شاه ترکان نیایش گرفت is a nominal phrase 'ابر شاه ترکان' ('the lofty/illustrious king of the Turks') followed by 'نیایش گرفت' ('took/received worship/prayer'); here 'گرفت' again functions as 'received' and 'ترکان' post-modifies 'شاه' as a genitive-like group ('king of the Turks'). 'ابر' is used poetically as an epithet meaning lofty/lofty‑like.
segments.s167-g7: که‌ای شاه پیروز is a vocative/exclamatory address to the king: 'که‌ای' functions as an archaic/exclamatory 'O' (calling someone), followed by 'شاه پیروز' 'victorious king'. The coordination 'و' that follows links this address to a subsequent optative clause.
segments.s167-g8: زمانه مبادا ز تو یادگار is an optative/negative-wish construction: 'مبادا' = 'may it not be / lest'; 'زمانه' ('in the course of time' or 'the age') sets the frame, and 'ز تو یادگار' ('a memorial/memento from you') is the nominal phrase that 'مبادا' denies—i.e. 'may there never be a memorial of you in time.'
segments.s167-g297: "ز" is the poetic form of "از" and means "from"; the phrase orders the agent before the verb so literally 'from the wise I heard a tale' but in natural English: 'I heard a tale from a wise man.' "شنیدم" is the past tense first-person verb 'I heard.' "یکی داستان" is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/one tale.'
segments.s167-g298: Here "خرد" (lit. 'wisdom' or 'the wise one') functions as the agent/subject notion 'the sage/the wise' and "شد" is the past/coprogressive 'became/was' used with the predicate that follows. "بران" = 'about/on that' (referring back to the tale). "نیز همداستان" = 'also of the same mind' — i.e. 'likewise agreed about it.' The clause means the wise man also came to the same opinion regarding that story.
segments.s167-g299: "که" introduces a result/subordinate clause here ('so that' / 'that'). "آهسته" is an adverb 'slowly' placed before the affected noun "دل" ('heart' = seat of feeling). "کم پشیمان" means 'a little regretful' ("کم" modifying the adjective), and "شود" is the present/subjunctive/modal copula 'may become' or 'become' used to express the resulting change of state.
segments.s167-g300: "هم" = 'also/likewise' links this clause to the previous one. "آشفته را" = 'the disturbed/distressed (one)', with "را" marking the definite person (emphatic/definite object/topic). "هوش درمان شود" is a copular/result construction: "هوش" (sense/awareness/wit) + "درمان" (cure/remedy) + "شود" ('becomes') — 'the wit is cured' = 'one's reason/awareness is restored.' Word order follows Persian nominal-predicate pattern.
segments.s167-g301: This is a nominal sentence: شتاب و بدی کار lists two nouns 'haste and the evil of action' as the subject. آهرمنست is the predicate: آهرمن + ـست ('is Ahriman') — the copula/stative‑ending is fused to the name, i.e. 'are (the work) of Ahriman' or 'belong to Ahriman'. The structure is typical of moralizing lines in classical Persian.
segments.s167-g302: This is another nominative formula with parallel possessive relations: پشیمانی جان = 'remorse is the soul's' (جان postposed to show possession), و links the clauses, رنج تنست = 'and suffering is the body's' where تنست = تن + است (body + 'is') acting as predicate 'is of the body' or simply 'is bodily.' The copula is again fused/omitted in ordinary position (poetic compression).
segments.s167-g303: سری here means 'a head/leader' (a person) and را is the definite/object marker. که introduces a relative clause 'who/which'. باشی is a subjunctive/present form 'be' used in the relative sense 'who is' (classical/poetic subjunctive form). بدو = 'to him/for him' and پادشا = 'king' is the predicate of that relative clause: 'the head who is to him a king' (i.e. 'one who is a king in his eyes').
segments.s167-g304: به تیزی بریدن is a verbal noun phrase: به تیزی = 'with swiftness/haste' (به + تیزی), بریدن = infinitive/gerund 'to cut/slay' (here object: 'to behead/cut off'). نبینم is first person present negative of دیدن used idiomatically 'I do not see/consider', and روا is a predicative adjective 'permissible/fitting'. So the clause means 'I do not consider it permissible to cut off (swiftly)'.
segments.s167-g433: که here is a subordinating conjunction introducing a purpose/result clause (“that/so that”). این را uses the demonstrative این plus the object marker را, marking “this” as the direct object. بجایی is an adverbial/locative phrase (بجا) meaning “to a suitable/some place” (poetic usage); بریدش is a compound verb form (برید + ـش) meaning “cut off/send away him/it” with ـش = ‘him’. The second که begins the subordinate clause that defines the destination (“to a place that…”).
segments.s167-g434: نباشد is a negative third-person present/subjunctive form meaning “may there not be / there should be no…”; it links back to که to express the wished-for condition. ورا combines و (toward/for) + را here functioning as a dative/goal “to him.” یار is used in the sense “companion/ally,” and و (conjunction) links it to the final noun فریادرس “one who answers cries / rescuer.”
segments.s167-g435: سرش را places سر (head) with the possessive enclitic ش = ‘his’ plus the object marker را, marking the direct object “his head.” ببرید here is an imperative/perfective form addressed to plural/official agents (“cut off” / “you (pl) cut off”), and یکسر modifies the action as ‘entirely / at once’. ز before تن is the poetic genitive/prepositional ز = ‘from,’ so یکسر ز تن = “entirely from the body.”
segments.s167-g436: تنش = تن + ـش ‘his body’ (possessive). کرگسان is the plural agent noun “vultures/scavengers.” را is the object marker attached to the noun phrase that follows the verb. بپوشد is a jussive/optative third-person singular (classical-style form) meaning “let (them) cover / may (it) be covered”; here it governs کفن as the thing used to cover. کفن is the covering/shroud noun functioning as the direct object of بپوشد.
segments.s167-g437: بباید is an impersonal modal (“it must / ought”) introducing an obligation or decree. که introduces the content-clause. خون سیاوش is a genitive construction with خون modified by the name سیاوش (“Siavash’s blood”). زمین is the subject/scene (the earth/ground) expected to react to the blood; word order puts the theme before the verb clause that follows.
segments.s167-g438: This segment strings two negative jussive/present forms and their objects. نبوید is the negative present/subjunctive of بوییدن (“may not/should not give off a scent / be smelled”), and نروید is a negative present/subjunctive of روییدن (here “may not sprout / grow”). گیا (a poetic/collective noun ‘plants, vegetation’) is the object of روییدن (i.e., plants sprouting), and روز کین (“day of vengeance”) is a temporal adjunct specifying when this ought not to occur. The negatives apply to both verbs as part of the same desiderative clause.
segments.s167-g439: همی is a narrative adverb signaling continued or repeated action (“kept / was continually”). تاختندش is the past/plural verb with enclitic ـش = ‘him’, meaning “they charged at / attacked him.” پیاده کشان is an agentive compound: پیاده ‘on foot’ + کشان (a deverbal/agentive suffix) denoting the attacking foot-soldiers or those who pull/drag on foot (i.e., infantry).
segments.s167-g440: چنان is a demonstrative/adverb meaning “so / thus / such,” setting up a comparison. روزبانان is an agent noun (pl.) meaning “day-keepers/announcers/heralds” (here a group descriptor). مردم is the noun “people,” and کشان again is the agentive/participle element attached to form a compound meaning “slayers/killers of people” (مردم‌کشان). Together the phrase compares the attackers to those who slaughter men.
segments.s167-g393: یکی را (yekī rā) is an indefinite direct object: یکی = 'one/someone' and را is the definite/object marker attached after it, marking it as the patient. به چاه places that object into a location (به here = 'into/toward' + چاه = 'pit/well'). افگند is the classical past (3rd singular) of افکندن, 'he threw/cast'; بی‌گناه is the adjective 'innocent' placed after the verb for poetic emphasis and modifies یکی.
segments.s167-g394: یکی introduces a second indefinite person ('another one'). با + کله literally 'with (the) head' and here indicates how the person was presented (i.e. by/with the head — a violent/display sense). برنشاند is a past causative/putting verb (برنشاندن) meaning 'set/place/display' (3rd singular). به گاه is a prepositional/time/occasional phrase; گاه here = 'occasion/place' (poetically 'as a spectacle' or 'on the occasion'), linked to برنشاند.
segments.s167-g395: سرانجام is an adverb meaning 'in the end/finally'. هر دو = 'both' (هر functioning as 'each' in the fixed phrase هر دو). به خاک اندرند is a stative locative: به + خاک = 'into/in the earth' and اندرند (present/pluperfect-form used statively) means 'they are inside/they lie (buried)'.
segments.s167-g396: ز اختر is a poetic agent/causal phrase: ز (here = 'because of/by') + اختر 'star' (used idiomatically for fate/the heavens). به چنگ = 'into the clutch/grasp' (به = 'into/toward' + چنگ 'claw, grasp'), مغاک 'abyss/gulf' names the thing holding them, and اندرند repeats the stative 'they are (within)'. The line says their end is due to the stars and they lie in the abyss's grasp.
segments.s167-g397: شنیدی is 2nd-person past/perfect ('you heard' or 'have you heard') used as an introducing question. که introduces the content clause 'that/what'. از آفریدون گرد is a genitive/partitive phrase: از = 'from/of', آفریدون = the name Manūčihr's ancestor Afrīdūn, and گرد here means 'the circle/retinue/those around him' (poetic usage meaning 'those about X').
segments.s167-g398: ستمگاره is an epithet meaning 'the oppressor' and precedes the name ضحاک (Zahhāk). تازی is an appositive/epithet 'the Arab' qualifying Zahhāk. چه برد is the interrogative content 'what did (he) take/do/bring about' with برد the past of بردن 'to take/carry/bring' (3rd singular); the subject is Zahhāk and is omitted because it was just named.
segments.s167-g399: همان here works as a linking demonstrative/adverb meaning 'the same' or 'likewise', pointing to a parallel example. از introduces the source 'from Manūchihir' (آفریدون in the previous sentence; here منوچهر is another king). شاه بزرگ is an appositive 'the great king' modifying the name.
segments.s167-g400: چه آمد به is an idiom meaning 'what came upon/what befell' (چه = 'what', آمد = past of آمدن 'to come', with به marking the affected party). سلم و به تور are the two names 'Salm and Tur' joined by و. سترگ follows as an epithet ('mighty/strong' in context) describing them; syntactically it is postpositive and qualifies the preceding names.
segments.s167-g305: ببندش همی دار combines two short imperatives: ببندش (ببند + -ش) = “bind/tie him” (ببند is a command, -ش = object 'him/it'), followed by همی دار where همی is a classical particle indicating continuing/ongoing action (“still/keep on”) and دار is an imperative ‘hold/keep’ (here used as a verb meaning ‘keep him bound’). تا روزگار begins a temporal clause: تا = “until” and روزگار = “time/fate.” The segment therefore directs to bind and continue holding until a point in time/fate arrives.
segments.s167-g306: برین is written as the sequence بر + ین (بر = ‘on/upon’, ین = orthographic variant of این ‘this’), so برین بد = “this bad/evil” (literally ‘upon/this bad’). ترا is the enclitic form of ‘to you’ (تو + را) and باشد here expresses a subjunctive/optative sense “may it be / become”; آموزگار = “teacher” or moral instructor. Together: “until fate makes this evil a teacher for you.”
segments.s167-g307: چو = ‘when’; باد here is literal metaphorical ‘wind/breeze’ (not the optative particle); خرد = ‘wisdom’; بر دلت = بر + دل + ت (بر = ‘upon’, دل = ‘heart’, -ت = your) — the possessive suffix is attached to دل. بروزد is a present/future-oriented verb from روزیدن meaning ‘to dawn/appear/strike’, so the clause means ‘when the breeze of wisdom dawns upon your heart’ (i.e., when you have wisdom).
segments.s167-g308: از ان پس = ‘from that time after’ (از = ‘from’, ان = ‘that’, پس = ‘after/then’). ورا is the old object form ‘to him/that one’; سربریدن is the infinitive ‘to behead’ (object/act), and سزد is the present/present-subjunctive impersonal ‘it befits / is fitting’. The whole clause: ‘after that, it is fitting to behead him.’
segments.s167-g309: بفرمای here is a reporting/imperative verb from فرمود/فرما — in context it introduces the order (‘he said’ / ‘he commanded’). بند is the command-content ‘bind’ (here nominal/imperative sense ‘bind/fetter’), و = ‘and’, تو = vocative/subject ‘you’, تندی = ‘haste’ and مکن = negative imperative ‘do not’. Together: ‘He said, “Bind (him),” and you do not be hasty.’
segments.s167-g310: که here introduces a causal clause ‘for/because’. تندی = ‘haste’; پشیمانی = ‘regret’; آرد is present/future ‘brings’ (آوردن root); به بن = ‘to the root/base’ (به = ‘to/for’, بن = ‘root/foundation’), an idiom meaning ‘deeply’ or ‘to the very foundation’. So: ‘for haste brings regret to the root’ — a warning that rashness leads to deep regret.
segments.s167-g311: چه here is an interrogative/exclamatory ‘why/what (that one would…)’. بری in this context functions as a verbal notion ‘to cut off/sever’ (the form or sense is archaic/poetic), سری = ‘head’ (سَر with possessive/accusative construction), را = object marker, همی = an emphatic/classical particle (here ‘indeed/just’ or continuous sense), بی‌گناه = ‘innocent’. So the clause asks ‘why would you sever the head of one who is innocent?’
segments.s167-g312: که = ‘for/since’; کاووس و رستم = the proper names Kavus and Rostam; بود = past copula ‘were’; کینه خواه is a compound where کینه = ‘grudge/hatred’ and خواه (here) = ‘seeking/desirous of’ (verbal/adjectival use), so کینه خواه = ‘intent on vengeance’ or ‘seeking a grudge’. The clause explains motive: ‘for Kavus and Rostam were intent on revenge.’
segments.s167-g289: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause ('that'); word order is Subject (خون سیاوش) + verb (بریزد) at the end, a common classical/Persian order. 'بریزد' here is an optative/subjunctive/present form meaning 'may be spilled / be shed' (a wished-for or destined result). 'به درد' means 'for/on account of a pain' (درد = emotional sorrow).
segments.s167-g290: 'کزو' (= 'from which') refers back to همان 'درد' just mentioned. 'داشت' is past 'had'. 'درد دل' is a compound meaning 'heart‑pain/heartache' (درد qualified by دل). 'اندر نبرد' = 'in/within battle' (اندر = in, نبرد = battle). The whole segment gives the source/character of the pain.
segments.s167-g291: 'ز پیران' = 'of/from the elders' (poetic 'ز' = از). 'یکی بود' = 'there was one' (بود past copula). 'کهتر به سال' is an adjectival phrase 'smaller/younger in years' — classical order places the adjective after the verb here. The phrase as a whole: 'one of the elders was younger in age.'
segments.s167-g292: The earlier 'بود' governs a small predicate phrase: 'برادر بد او را' functions as 'he had an ill/bad brother' where او را indicates the person affected (dative/possessive sense in older usage). 'و فرخ همال' adds 'and a farrokh (fortunate) porter' — two predicative nouns/adjectives joined by و.
segments.s167-g293: 'کجا' here = 'there/where' (locative). The clause uses inverted classical order: 'پیلسم بود نام جوان' literally 'Pilasm was the name of the young (man)' — English: 'a young man named Pilasm.' 'بود' is the past copula linking name and person.
segments.s167-g294: 'یکی' = 'one' / 'a certain (man)'; 'پرهنر' is an adjective 'full of skill' (skilled), 'بود' = 'was' (past), and 'روشن‌روان' is a compound adjective meaning 'bright‑souled' or 'cheerful/spiritful'. Together: 'a certain one was skilled and bright‑souled.'
segments.s167-g295: 'چنین' = 'thus/so'. 'گفت' is the past verb 'said' (the provided senses list is noun-like but here it operates as the verb 'said'); word order is topical/poetic: indirect objects 'مر' (to me) and 'شاه را' (to the king) appear before the subject 'پیلسم' at the clause end, a common inversion in classical verse.
segments.s167-g296: 'که' introduces the reported content ('that'). 'این شاخ را' is marked with the object marker را; classical Persian often omits an explicit copula, so the predicate is elliptic: 'this branch (is) بار دردست و غم' — 'a load/burden in the hand and sorrow.' 'دردست' literally 'at hand / in possession.'
segments.s167-g265: همی + گشت is a classical continuous/perfective sequence: همی (a habitual/continuous particle) gives an ongoing imperfective sense to گشت (past-stem ‘turned/rolled’), so the clause means ‘he kept turning/rolling’. بر خاک is a simple prepositional phrase (‘on the ground’). نیزه به دست is an instrumental-style phrase ‘spear in (his) hand’ (به‌دست = with/in the hand) placed after the locative phrase.
segments.s167-g266: This clause contains a nominal phrase plus a transitive past verb: گروی زره is the actor/agent phrase (here functioning like ‘the strap/fastening of the armor’), دست او را is the object with را marking definiteness/accusative (‘his hand’), and ببست is the 3rd-person past transitive ‘tied/bound’. The word order is agent – object – verb, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s167-g267: نهادند is the 3rd-person plural past of نهادن ‘to place/set’, so ‘they placed’. بر گردنش is a prepositional phrase ‘on/around his neck’ (گردن + possessive ش = ‘his neck’). پالهنگ is the direct object placed there (here ‘leopard-skin’/‘leopard’ as covering).
segments.s167-g268: This clause describes the binding: دو دست ‘two hands’ is the object, از پس پشت ‘from behind the back’ indicates that the hands were tied behind him, بسته is a past participle/adjective ‘bound/tied’, and چو سنگ is a simile ‘like a stone’. The sequence places location/adverbial phrase between noun and adjective, as in Persian narrative.
segments.s167-g269: دوان here functions as a verbal/adjectival notion of running/flowing (i.e., ‘streaming’), modifying خون ‘blood’. بران (بر + آن contracted) means ‘over/on that’ and links to چهره ‘face’; ارغوان is an adjectival color ‘crimson/purple’. The phrase describes blood flowing over a crimson face.
segments.s167-g270: چنان روز is a demonstrative phrase ‘such a day/scene’; نادیده is a past-participial adjective ‘unseen’ (literally ‘not seen’), and چشم جوان is a genitival phrase ‘the young eye’ (چشم = eye/sight, جوان = young, adjectival). The sequence means roughly ‘a sight/day unseen by the young eye’ (i.e., the like of which no young person had seen).
segments.s167-g271: برفتند is the 3rd-person plural past ‘they went/moved’. سوی Siaavosh indicates direction ‘toward Siyâvash’. گرد here functions as an adverbial/particle meaning ‘around/about’, so the clause expresses movement converging on Siyâvash from all sides.
segments.s167-g272: This is a verbless descriptive clause with بود (past of ‘to be’) supplying the copula: پس پشت و پیش lists spatial positions ‘behind (پس/پشت) and before/front (پیش)’, سپه ‘the army’ is the subject, بود گرد = ‘was around’ (بود = was, گرد = about/around). The clause states that the army lay all around, both in front and behind.
segments.s167-g185: ز (preposition) = 'from' introduces origin ('from Iran'). یکی before a noun here has the classical sense 'a certain / one' (indefinite introducer). لشکر (army) is the direct noun; آرد is the archaic/persianized present/future form of آوردن ('to bring, to send') used here as 'comes / brings'. به + کین is a prepositional phrase 'for vengeance' showing purpose. Word order is typical older Persian: prepositional origin (ز ایران) + indefinite specifier (یکی) + noun (لشکر) + verb (آرد) + purpose phrase (به کین).
segments.s167-g186: پرآشوب is an adjective/participial compound meaning 'full of turmoil / tumultuous'. گردد is the older third-person singular present/subjunctive (from گشتن) here functioning as 'becomes' (resultative). سراسر زمین is a locative/extent phrase 'throughout the land' placed after the verb for emphasis. Together they say the result: the land becomes tumultuous.
segments.s167-g187: پی before a noun here means 'after / in pursuit of'. رخش فرخ زمین is a classical compounded epithet/name for the steed (literally 'Rakhsh, fortunate of the earth'); the adjective فرخ modifies رخش and زمین is part of the epithet. بسپرد is the past/preterite or archaic perfective of سپردن ('to entrust, send, dispatch') meaning 'he sent/let loose'. Word order places the pursuit marker + named steed before the verb.
segments.s167-g188: به توران = 'toward Turan' (direction). کسی را is the object-marker construction ('anyone / someone' with را marking it as the affected/definite object). به کس نشمرد is an idiomatic negative: نشمرد (past/preterite or imperfect of شمردن) here means 'did not reckon/consider'; the duplicated structure کسی را به کس lit. 'anyone as anyone' signals 'considered no one as anyone' — i.e. he did not count/esteem or he spared/recognized no one. The two 'به' phrases frame direction and the internal comparison respectively.
segments.s167-g189: به کین من = 'for my vengeance' (به marks purpose/attitude, کین = vengeance, من = possessive 'my'). امروز 'today / from now on'. تا introduces a temporal limit 'until'; رستخیز here is the classical noun 'resurrection' (often used hyperbolically to mean the final reckoning or uprising). The whole phrase sets the time-span for the speaker's vow.
segments.s167-g190: نبینی is the negative second-person singular present/subjunctive/imperative-prohibitive form of دیدن ('you will not / do not see'). جز 'except'. از introduces source/listing 'of / but'. گرز و شمشیر تیز = a coordinated noun phrase 'maces and sharp swords' (تیز modifies شمشیر). The clause means 'you will see nothing except maces and sharp swords.'
segments.s167-g191: برین گفتها = 'in/on these words' (بر + این in literary form = 'on these' + گفتها 'words/sayings'). بر تو here is a dative/target 'upon you / for you'. دل‌سخت کن is an imperative: دل‌سخت (adjective 'hard-hearted / resolute') + کن (imperative 'make/do') = 'harden your heart' or 'become resolute'. The phrase is an instruction prompted by the preceding speech.
segments.s167-g192: تن 'the body / yourself' is the patient/topic; از introduces what should be given up ('from / of'). ناز و آرام = 'softness/pampering and comfort/peace'. پردخت is an older verbal noun/verb form related to پرداختن (to give up/spend/turn away) used imperatively here with کن: پردخت کن = 'make (the) giving up' i.e. 'forsake' or 'abandon' (the available sense lists give 'payment' or 'finish' — here the sense in context is 'expend / give up'). The final کن is the imperative force 'do this'.
segments.s167-g345: که here introduces a subordinate admonitory clause ('that' / marking the clause); چندین is an adverb meaning 'many times' or 'repeatedly'; به + خون سیاوش is a prepositional phrase literally 'on/over Siavash's blood' with به marking direction/target; مپیچ is a negative imperative formed from پیچیدن with the negative prefix مـ (i.e. 'do not turn/linger/wind'), so the whole segment reads roughly 'do not keep turning (i.e. dwelling) over Siavash's blood'.
segments.s167-g346: که again begins a subordinate clause giving a reason or consequence; آرام خوار is a two‑word descriptive phrase ('calm/quiet' + 'despised/worthless') functioning as a nominative group; آید is a present/subjunctive 'comes' (here: 'will come' / 'will appear'); اندر بسیچ is a locative 'into/among the multitudes' (اندر = 'in/among', بسیچ = 'a multitude/host'). Grammatically the clause states the consequence: 'that the calm/despised will come among the host.'
segments.s167-g347: به گفتار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/according to the speech/words of...'; گرسیوز is a proper name (Garsivaz); رهنمای is a noun 'guide' or 'adviser' in apposition to Garsivaz. The whole segment means 'by the counsel/words of Garsivaz the adviser.'
segments.s167-g348: برآرای is an imperative or hortative verb meaning 'array/prepare/put in order' (classical/poetic imperative form); و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking two imperatives; بردار here is another imperative 'lift/take away' (from برداشتن); دشمن is the direct object 'enemy'; ز جای is a prepositional phrase 'from his place' with poetic preposition ز = 'from' and جای = 'place/position.'
segments.s167-g349: زدی is a past tense verb 'you struck' or 'you set' (here 'you set/laid'); دام is the object 'a snare/trap'; و connects clauses; دشمن گرفتـی is 'you seized/captured the enemy' with گرفتی as the past second‑person perfect of گرفتن. Grammatically this lists two actions performed by the subject ('you').
segments.s167-g350: ز ایران برآید is a clause 'from Iran will arise/appear' with ز = 'from' and برآید a present/subjunctive 'arise/appear'; یکی‌های is an indefinite/partitive expression 'one(s)' or 'a certain number' (here 'one or some'); و is 'and'; هوی is an interjection/exclamation often representing a shout or cry (an onomatopoeic war‑cry). Together the clause says that from Iran will come certain people/cries rising up.
segments.s167-g351: سزا نیست is an idiomatic predicate meaning 'it is not fitting' or 'this is not right'; این را uses the demonstrative این with the object marker را to mark 'this' as the object; گرفتن is the infinitive 'to take / to seize' functioning as the complement of سزا نیست; به دست is a prepositional phrase 'into the hand' or 'into the power' (به + دست). The clause means roughly 'It is not right to take this into (one's) hands.'
segments.s167-g352: دل بدسگالان is a genitive construction 'the hearts of the ill‑fated' (دل = 'heart', بدسگالان = plural adjective/noun 'ill‑fated / ill‑omened people'); بباید is a modal auxiliary meaning 'must/ought to' (classical form of باید); شکست is a noun/verb 'breaking' or 'defeat' used here as the result—together 'the hearts of the ill‑fated must be broken' (i.e. they must be defeated/broken in spirit).
segments.s167-g377: بدو گفت is a short clause meaning “he said to him”: بدو is the prepositional pronoun (بـ + او) ‘to him/unto him’ and گفت is the past-tense verb ‘said’ (3rd‑person). کای is a vocative particle ‘O,’ followed by the epithet پرهنر (an adjective composed with the prefix پر‑, ‘full/very’, + هنر ‘skill/culture’) modifying شهریار ‘king/sovereign’; the vocative phrase addresses the addressee.
segments.s167-g378: This is a direct question addressed to the sovereign. چرا is the interrogative ‘why’; کرد is the past of کردن ‘to do/make’ (here ‘made/caused’); خواهی is the 2nd‑person present/future form of خواستن ‘to want’ used emphatically in classical/poetic style (the combination yields a confrontational rhetorical question); مرا is the object pronoun ‘me’; خاکسار is a noun meaning ‘humble supplicant’ or ‘contrite one’ and functions as the predicative object.
segments.s167-g379: دلت را is ‘your heart’ (دل + 2nd‑person suffix ت, plus را as an object/contrast marker). چرا بستی is a past interrogative ‘why did you bind/close’ (بستی is 2nd‑person past of بستن). اندر is the preposition ‘in/within,’ and فریب is the noun ‘deception/delusion’; together the phrase asks why one has closed one’s heart within deception.
segments.s167-g380: همی is an adverb often meaning ‘still/continually’ or an emphatic marker here preceding the clause. از بلندی is a prepositional phrase ‘from (or out of) the height’ (از + بلندی). نبینی is present/subjunctive with negative prefix ن‑ meaning ‘you do not see’ (or ‘you cannot see’); نشیب is the noun ‘descent/low place’ and is placed after the verb in this poetic word order: literally ‘ever from the height you do not see the descent.’
segments.s167-g381: This is an imperative negative: مبر is the negative imperative ‘do not take’ (from برداشتن/گرفتن in the classical poetic imperative form). سر تاجداران is the object phrase ‘the heads of the crown‑bearers’ (سر ‘head’ followed by تاجداران ‘those who wear crowns’), and بی‌گناه follows as a postpositive adjective ‘innocent,’ qualifying those crown‑bearers.
segments.s167-g382: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ‘for/because.’ نپسندد is the negative present/subjunctive or jussive third‑person form ‘(he/it) will not approve’ or ‘does not approve.’ این داور هور و ماه is a nominal phrase: این ‘this’ + داور ‘judge/arbiter’ with the epithet هور و ماه (‘sun and moon’ or ‘splendor and moon’) appended for emphasis—together, ‘this judge of sun and moon’ (a lofty/divine judge).
segments.s167-g383: سیاوش is the proper name ‘Siyâvash.’ که is a relative pronoun ‘who/that.’ بگذاشت is the classical past form meaning ‘he left/abandoned’ or more literally ‘he caused to leave’ in context; here it conveys that Siyâvash departed or gave up ایران‌زمین ‘the land of Iran’ (ایران‌زمین is a compound noun ‘land of Iran’).
segments.s167-g384: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle ‘still/ever/even’; از جهان here means ‘from the world’ or ‘in the world’ (جهان as ‘this life / the world’); بر تو is the prepositional phrase ‘upon you / to you’; کرد is the past of کردن ‘did/made’ and together کرد آفرین means ‘gave praise’ or ‘praised’ (آفرین as ‘praise/acclamation’). The clause says that Siyâvash praised you from among the world/people.
segments.s167-g49: ترا (ت + را) is the object 'you' marked by the object clitic را (here split into two tokens for metrical/orthographic reasons). کردم آگه is a past-tense clause 'I made (you) aware' — کردَم is 'I did/made' and آگه is a predicative/adjectival complement 'aware/informed'. ز introduces the source/reason 'from/by', and دیدار خوی + ش is a genitival/possessive sequence 'the meeting/sight of (his/one's own)'; here the possessive suffix ش attaches to خوی (split in the tokenization), so the whole phrase gives the cause/reason by which the addressee was made aware.
segments.s167-g50: ازین پس is an adverbial phrase 'from this [time] onward / henceforth' (از = 'from', ین = orthographic variant of این). بپیچی is a 2nd-person singular jussive/imperative-form verb with the ب- prefix ('you should/you must turn/withdraw'); ز introduces the ablative 'from' and کردار خوی + ش is 'deed/conduct of his/your own' (خوی + ش forming the possessive), so the clause addresses the addressee and commands that they desist/turn away from their own conduct.
segments.s167-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the simple past (3rd person singular) 'he heard'. The proper name افراسیاب is the subject (typical VSO order), and این سخن is the object 'these/this words/speech'.
segments.s167-g52: برو here functions as a poetic/adverbial particle meaning 'thereupon' (not the literal 'go'); تازه شد is 'became new' (شد past of 'become' with تازه 'new/fresh' as predicate), and روزگار کهن is the noun phrase 'the ancient/old time(s)' serving as the subject that undergoes the change. Word order is poetic but straightforward: adverbial particle + predicate + subject phrase.
segments.s167-g53: به marks the indirect object 'to/for' and introduces the addressee گرسیوز (Garsivaz). از خشم is an ablative of cause 'from/out of anger'. پاسخ نداد is a past negative: داد ('give') with negative prefix ن- → 'did not give (a reply)' (i.e. 'did not answer'). Word order places the indirect object and reason before the verbal phrase for emphasis.
segments.s167-g54: دلش is 'his heart' (دل + possessive suffix ش). گشت is the past of گشتن used as a copular/auxiliary 'became/turned (into)'. پرآتش is a compound adjective 'full of fire / fiery'. و connects the second clause, and سر چو باد is a simile 'his head like the wind' (چو = 'like', باد = 'wind'); the copula is omitted in the second half, as is common in Persian poetry.
segments.s167-g55: بفرمود is an honorific past-tense verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that / that', and برکشیدند is the past plural 'they raised/lifted' (برکشیدن = to lift up, here applied to instruments). نای is the direct object 'the ney' (reed flute). The clause therefore reports an order that the musicians lift the ney.
segments.s167-g56: همان begins the following enumerative list meaning 'the same/that very', and the items are coordinated by و: سنج (here an instrument, 'cymbal' in the poetic musical list), شیپور 'trumpet', و هندی درای — an adjectival/nominal phrase. درای is an archaic/poetic form related to دارا/دارای 'possessing' or an attributive, so هندی درای likely qualifies an Indian instrument or person in the same list. The whole segment is a nominal list of instruments/objects called for by the command.
segments.s167-g369: خردمند‌گر is a conditional phrase: خردمند (the wise) + گر (if).
