It sets up an 'if' clause: 'if the wise [one] ...'. مردم بدگمان follows the conditional subject and object: مردم (the people) marked as the thing suspected, and بدگمان is an adjective meaning 'suspicious' describing the speaker's attitude toward مردم.
segments.s167-g370: نداند is 3rd-person past/present sense 'to know' (here functionally 'does/does not know' in classical narrative). کسی follows in inverted order to yield the sense 'no one' (kisi here functions as 'no one' in negative contexts). چاره آسمان is the object: چاره 'remedy/counsel' and آسمان 'Heaven' (i.e. the will/counsel of Heaven). Word order is inverted for emphasis: 'no one knows Heaven's counsel.'
segments.s167-g371: فرنگیس is the subject (proper name). بشنید is a simple past 3rd-singular 'she heard'. رخ را بخست is a transitive clause: رخ 'face' + را (definite object marker) + بخست (past of بستن, 'closed/veiled'); the verb بخست (with poetic b- prefix) means 'she covered/veiled her face' and follows the object for emphasis.
segments.s167-g372: میان را 'the midriff/waist' takes را as definite object. به + زنار خونین is an instrumental/means phrase 'with a blood-stained girdle' (به here indicating the instrument/with). ببست is past 3rd-singular of بستن 'she bound/tied'. The sequence shows two coordinated past actions: she veiled her face and bound her waist.
segments.s167-g373: پیاده functions adverbially 'on foot' and precedes the verb. بیامد is past 3rd-singular 'came'. به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the king' (به + نزدیک + شاه), indicating motion toward the king; word order places the goal after the verb.
segments.s167-g374: به خون رنگ داده is a participial phrase modifying رخساره: رنگ داده (lit. 'having given color' = 'stained/tinged') plus به + خون indicating the agent/instrument 'with blood' (به here = 'by/with'). دو رخساره ماه is the noun phrase 'two moon-like faces' (دو numeral + رخساره 'cheek/face' + ماه 'moon' used as a simile). Together: 'her two moon-like cheeks stained with blood.'
segments.s167-g375: به پیش پدر is a locative phrase 'in the presence of / before the father' (به + پیش + پدر). شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became/was'. پر از درد و باک is a predicate: پر 'full' + از (of) + درد 'pain/sorrow' and باک 'fear' — together 'filled with pain and fear.' The clause describes her emotional condition before her father.
segments.s167-g376: خروشان is an adverbial/participial manner 'roaring/tumultuously' modifying the following action. به سر 'upon (her) head' uses به as a locative/prepositional particle. بر همی ریخت is a verbal cluster: بر (on/upon) + همی (an archaic/poetic continuous marker often used in Shahnameh) + ریخت (past of ریختن, 'poured/Threw about'), giving 'poured/strewn upon.' خاک is the object 'dust/earth.' Altogether: 'with loud cries she cast dust upon her head.'
segments.s167-g25: دلاور is the nominal subject 'the hero/valiant one' (here used as a noun). 'سه اسپ تگاور' is a numeral + noun + modifier phrase: 'سه' (three) precedes the noun 'اسپ' (horse/steed), and 'تگاور' functions as an attributive descriptor ('war‑steed' / 'warrior‑type') modifying اسپ. The clause ends with the past verb بخواست (بخواست), 'he desired/asked for', placed after its object as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s167-g26: همی is an archaic/prosaic proclitic indicating continuous or habitual action ('continually/still'); it precedes the verb تاخت ('galloped/rushed'). یکسر functions adverbially 'without stop/continually' and modifies the following time phrase 'شب و روز' (night and day). راست here is an adverbial 'straight/direct' (i.e. straight on); the whole clause thus describes continuous, direct riding through night and day.
segments.s167-g27: چهارم ('the fourth') is the subject noun. بیامد is the simple past 'came' (3rd person). The prepositional phrase به درگاه شاه ('to the king's court') follows the verb and marks the destination; به + noun denotes movement toward/into the royal presence.
segments.s167-g28: پر از ... is the standard Persian pattern 'full of X' where پر ('full') combines with the preposition از (here 'of') followed by a noun phrase: 'بد روان' = 'evil/ill disposition' (بد modifying روان). The conjunction و links a parallel noun phrase 'زبان پرگناه' where the adjective/compound پرگناه ('full of sin' / 'sinful') follows زبان ('speech/tongue'), yielding 'sinful speech' or 'a sinful tongue.'
segments.s167-g29: فراوان functions as an adverb 'at length/much' modifying the verb. بپرسیدش is a past narrative form 'he asked/questioned him' with the object enclitic -ش ('him'). The proper name افراسیاب appears after the verb (verb–object–subject order here), so the literal order is 'at length he asked him, Afrasiab.'
segments.s167-g30: چو is the conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. دیدش is 'saw him' (دید + enclitic -ش 'him'). پر از رنج repeats the 'full of' pattern: پر + از + رنج = 'full of suffering.' سر پرشتاب is a noun + adjective: سر 'head' + پرشتاب 'full of haste/very hurried', giving the idea 'and his head was full of haste' (i.e. he was agitated/urgent).
segments.s167-g31: This is a reported question: چرا ('why') + باشتاب آمدی ('did you come hastily' — باشتاب 'with haste', آمدی 2nd person past 'you came'). The speech is then attributed with گفت شاه ('said the king'), where the verb گفت ('said') precedes the subject شاه — a common narrative inversion after direct speech.
segments.s167-g32: چگونه ('how') introduces the second question; سپردی is the 2nd‑person past 'you entrusted/handed over.' چنین تند راه is a compact descriptive phrase: چنین ('such/so') + تند ('swift') modifying راه ('road/way'); the adjective cluster precedes the noun here for emphasis, so the clause asks how he could have sent/entrusted (someone or something) on so swift a path.
segments.s167-g89: چهارم شب is a temporal fronting: 'on the fourth night'. اندر بر + ماهروی is an archaic/prepositional cluster where ماهروی ('moon‑faced', used substantively = 'the moon‑faced one/beauty') follows the prepositions; together they mean 'in the presence of / before the moon‑faced one'. The word order is poetic (temporal phrase → prepositional phrase → noun).
segments.s167-g90: بخوان is the imperfective/present stem used with a past narrative sense in this context (verb + بود pattern) — here it functions like 'was singing/was chanting'. اندرون 'inside' marks location. بود is the past copula, producing a past‑continuous sense ('was ...'). با رنگ و بوی is an ablative/associative phrase 'with colour and scent' describing the atmosphere or the person.
segments.s167-g91: بلرزید is a simple past intransitive verb 'he/it trembled'. وز here is an archaic/prepositional item (in context meaning 'from' or 'out of') introducing the source ('from sleep'); the lemma list given doesn't include this sense so it is treated as an archaic preposition. خواب خیره بجست is a small clause: خواب 'sleep' + خیره 'dazed/stunned' (adjective) + بجست 'he sprang/leapt up' (past), so the sense is 'from sleep, (being) stunned, he leapt up.'
segments.s167-g92: خروشی is a noun 'a roar/outcry'. برآورد here functions as a past verb 'produced/let out' (literally 'brought forth'), so the clause means 'he produced a roar'. چون introduces a simile 'like', پیل 'elephant' is the vehicle, and مست 'drunk' is an adjective modifying پیل: 'like a drunken elephant.'
segments.s167-g93: همی داشت is a habitual/continuous past construction here meaning 'he kept/was holding'. اندر برش is a prepositional cluster with an object clitic (بر + ش) meaning 'before him / to him / at his side' (the tokenization shows برش as one item). خوب‌چهر is an adjective used substantively 'the fair‑faced (one)'. Together: 'he kept/held the fair‑faced one before him.'
segments.s167-g94: بدو is the prepositional clitic phrase 'to him/unto him'. گفت here is the past of گفتن 'said' (the supplied lemma senses list gave nouns, but the function here is the verb 'said'). شاها is the vocative 'O king!'. چبودت is an archaic fused question 'چه بودت' = 'what was (with you) / what ailed you' (چ = چه). ز is the preposition 'from/because of' and مهر 'love/affection' gives the cause: 'what ailed you — was it because of love?'
segments.s167-g95: خروشید is a past intransitive 'there was an outcry/they shouted/it roared'. و links clauses. شمعی here is the object 'a candle' (poetic adjective/noun form), and برافروختند is a past plural verb 'they kindled/they lit', so 'they lit a candle.'
segments.s167-g96: برش again appears as a prepositional/oblique element meaning 'for her / upon her' (the tokenization conflates pronoun-clitic forms). عود و عنبر are the incense items 'oud/agarwood and ambergris'. همی is a continuous/adverbial particle ('still/indeed' — here marking the action), and سوختند is past plural 'they burned' (i.e. they burned incense). Word order is topical: target ('for her') → what was burned → aspect particle → verb.
segments.s167-g249: همه (hemme) is the subject 'everyone' placed before the verb for emphasis. با سیاوش (bâ Siyâvash) is a prepositional phrase 'with/against Siyâvash' marking the opponent. گرفتند (gereftand) is a past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb 'they took/engaged in' (poetic use for 'took up/fought'); جنگ (jang) is the object 'war/battle'. Note the slightly inverted, poetic word order where the verb precedes its object.
segments.s167-g250: ندیدند (nadidand) is past tense, 3pl negative 'they did not see'. جای (jâye) here is figurative 'place/room' (i.e. 'no place for...'). فسون (fasun) is an archaic/poetic noun 'enchantment / trickery'. و درنگ (va darang) links with 'and hesitation/delay' — درنگ is 'delay, pause'. The clause therefore means 'they saw no room for trickery or hesitation.'
segments.s167-g251: کنون (konun) is a temporal adverb 'now'. خیره (khire) is an adverb/adjective 'staring, astonished' modifying گفتند. گفتند (goftand) is past 3rd-person plural 'they said' (here rendered with a sense of speaking/reporting). ما را (mâ râ) is the object 'us' plus the definite object marker را. کشند (kosh-and / keshand in classical) is an old-form 3rd-person plural (subjunctive/optative-like or reported-future) meaning 'they kill / will kill' in the reported speech — i.e. 'they said “they will kill us.”'
segments.s167-g252: بباید که (bebâyad ke) is an impersonal construction 'it must be / it is necessary that' introducing reported necessity. تنها (tanhâ) 'only' restricts the manner. به خون در (be khun dar) is a compact poetic expression meaning 'in/with blood' or 'by blood' — به + خون indicates instrument/manner. کشند (keshand) repeats the same verb-form as above: reported/subjunctive 'they will do / they will slay.' Together: 'It must be that they will do it, and they will do it only with blood.'
segments.s167-g253: بمان (bemân) is the 2nd-person singular imperative 'stay/remain'. تا (tâ) introduces an extent/temporal clause 'until'. ز ایرانیان (ze Irâniyân) means 'from the Iranians' (ز is the poetic genitive/ablative 'from/of'). دست برد (dast bard) is an idiomatic, condensed poetic phrase short for دست بردارند or دست بردند meaning 'take the hand away / desist / withdraw one's hand' (i.e. stop harming or interfering); the verb is presented in a compact, archaic poetic form.
segments.s167-g254: ببینند (bebinand) is a subjunctive/optative 3rd-person plural 'they may see' or 'they will see' in result clauses after تا. و (va) 'and' links two verbs. مشمر (mashmar) is a compact form meaning 'gird themselves / arm themselves' (from مشمر شدن or مشمر آوردن) — here parallel to ببینند as a jussive/subjunctive: 'they will see and gird themselves.' چنین کار خرد (chinân kâr-e kherad) is a noun phrase 'such a deed of wisdom' — کار 'deed/act', خرد 'wisdom' (i.e. a wise act).
segments.s167-g255: سیاوش (Siyâvash) is the subject/name. چنین گفت (chinân goft) is the formula 'thus said' — چنین 'thus' + گفت (past 3pl or reported 'said'). کین رای نیست (kin rây nist) is a negated nominal clause: کین 'vengeance, hatred' + رای 'opinion/counsel' — together 'a counsel of vengeance' — نیست 'is not', so 'this is not counsel of vengeance.'
segments.s167-g256: همان جنگ را (hamân jang râ) 'that same war' where را is the object/topic marker attaching emphasis to جنگ. مایه و پای نیست (mâye va pây nist) is a paired negation 'there is neither cause nor base' — مایه 'cause/resource' and پای 'base/foot/foundation' — followed by نیست 'is not/there is none'. The phrase means 'the war has neither justification nor basis.'
segments.s167-g225: چه is the fronted interrogative 'what'. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (third singular) following the question word. آن خردمند بسیار هوش is a noun phrase 'that very-wise sage' with خردمند (wise) as the head and بسیار هوش ('very/intensely intelligent') as a poetic epithet; the whole phrase is the subject of گفت.
segments.s167-g226: که introduces reported speech or a subordinate clause ('who/that') linking to what follows. با اختر بد is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with an ill/ill‑omened star' (اختر 'star', بد 'ill/unfavorable'); به مردی is the prepositional object 'toward/against a man'. مکوش is an archaic negative imperative/prohibitive from کوشیدن ('do not strive/attempt'); placed at the end, it gives the command 'do not contend'.
segments.s167-g227: چنین گفت is a formula 'thus he said' (چنین = thus; گفت = said). زان پس is a temporal phrase 'then/after that' (زان = from that; پس = after/then). به افراسیاب marks the indirect object 'to Afrasiab' with the preposition به.
segments.s167-g228: که‌ای here introduces the direct address inside reported speech; the sequence behaves like a vocative 'O ...' (که + ای is an archaic/poetic vocative flavor). پرهنر شاه is the addressee 'cultured/skilled king'. با جاه و آب is a terse attributive phrase 'with rank and (figurative) resources/glory' (آب used figuratively); the whole segment opens the speech directed at Afrasiab.
segments.s167-g229: چرا at the start is the question word 'why'. جنگ جوی is a compound/attributive expression 'war-seeking' (جوی here = seeker/one who seeks), modifying the implied subject 'you'. آمدی is the second‑person singular past 'you came', and با سپاه 'with an army' is the accompanying prepositional phrase.
segments.s167-g230: The second rhetorical question again begins with چرا 'why'. کشت is the noun/masdar sense 'killing' (object idea). خواهی is second‑person present/future 'you will/you want' (here 'do you wish/are you going to'), and م + را together represent the clitic object 'me' (م is the clitic form, را the definite-object marker). بی‌گناه is the predicative adjective 'innocent', so مرا بی‌گناه = 'me, innocent.'
segments.s167-g231: سپاه دو کشور is a genitive/attributive noun phrase 'the armies of two countries/lands'. پر از کین is a predicative phrase 'full of hatred/vengeance' (پر = full (of); از = of/from; کین = hatred/vengeance). کنی is the second‑person singular present subjunctive/optative from کردن used here as 'you make/you cause' and sits at the end as the main verb ('you cause/make ... full').
segments.s167-g232: زمان و زمین 'time and earth' are coordinated nouns serving as the direct object/topic. پر ز نفرین repeats the 'full of ...' construction in a poetic variant using ز (poetic form of از) — 'full of curse(s)'. کنی is again the second‑person verb 'you make/you fill', so the segment says 'you fill time and earth with curses.'
segments.s167-g73: همی is a classical/perhaps poetic continuous/progressive marker meaning 'kept on' or 'continually'. بارید is the past simple of باریدن ('to fall, to rain'); together همی بارید = 'kept raining/falling'. بر is the preposition 'on/upon' and کوه سیم is a noun-noun compound 'silver mountain' (کوه modified by سیم), so the whole phrase literally reads 'tears continually fell upon the silver mountain.'
segments.s167-g74: دو لاله is the subject 'two tulips' (a poetic image). ز here marks origin or source ('from'), خوشاب is 'sweet/pleasant water', شد is the past of شدن meaning 'became' or 'came to be', and به دو نیم means 'into two halves' (به + measure). Together: 'two tulips, from the sweet water, became into two halves' — i.e. they were split in two.
segments.s167-g75: همی again marks continuous/repeated action. کند is the present/past-form of کردن used here as 'does/tear(s)'; موی (object) means 'hair' and the object marker is omitted (classical style), so همی کند موی = 'he kept pulling his hair'. همی ریخت آب uses ریخت (from ریختن) with آب 'water' as a poetic image for tears: 'kept pouring/letting fall water (i.e. tears)'.
segments.s167-g76: ز here has a causal/ablative sense 'because of/from'. گفتار و کردار are a paired noun phrase 'words and deeds' and افراسیاب follows as the agent/possessor: 'from (or because of) Afrasiab's words and deeds.' The preposition z applies to both nouns in the coordination.
segments.s167-g77: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (be + 3rd-person enclitic), and گفت is the verb 'said' (past). کای is the vocative particle 'O', and شاه گردن‌فراز is a vocative epithet 'king, high-necked/proud-necked' (adjective after noun as an epithet). The phrase functions as direct address: 'He said to him: O proud-necked king...'.
segments.s167-g78: چه سازی is an idiomatic question 'what will you do?' where چه 'what' + سازی (from ساختن/سازیدن 'to make/do') asks about intended action. کنون زود are adverbs 'now, quickly' (کنون = now, زود = quickly/soon). بگشای is an imperative or hortative form of گشودن 'to open/reveal', and راز is the object 'secret' at the end: 'Now quickly open/reveal the secret.'
segments.s167-g79: پدر خود literally 'his own father' (خود adding emphasis/possession). دلی دارد literally 'has a heart' — an idiom meaning 'has sorrow' or 'is grieved'. از تو indicates the cause 'because of you' and به درد is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'in pain/afflicted'. Word order is classical: possessor phrase + idiomatic predicate + cause.
segments.s167-g80: از ایران here means 'from Iran' or 'of Iran' qualifying نیاری ('a beloved/lover'); سخن یاد کرد is a compound meaning 'spoke/mentioned (a story or word)' — یاد کردن with سخن as its object means 'to mention a tale/word'. So the segment reads 'he mentioned/spoke of an Iranian beloved.'
segments.s167-g129: نگر is an imperative (addressing the addressee) meaning 'consider' or 'look at.' تا چه introduces the indirect question 'what/how' that follows; here تا functions to link the verb to the subordinate question clause. باید is the modal 'must/ought' in the present/predictive sense, کنون is the temporal adverb 'now,' and ساختن is the infinitive 'to make/do/arrange' — together meaning 'what must now be done.' Word order is imperative + subordinating particle + question-word + modal + time + infinitive.
segments.s167-g130: سپه را is the definite direct object 'the army' marked by را. کجا is the interrogative 'where' introducing the location question; باید is again the modal 'must/ought,' and انداختن is an infinitive meaning 'to place / to set / to cast' here used as 'place/position.' The clause asks 'where the army ought to be placed.'
segments.s167-g131: سیاوش is the subject (proper name). ندانست is the past tense negative of 'to know' ('did not know'). زان is a prepositional/participle particle meaning 'of that/from that,' and کار او means 'that deed/affair (of his/that one).' The sequence gives 'Siyâvash did not know of that deed.'
segments.s167-g132: همی is an intensifier/adverb here ('indeed' / 'just'), راست is adjectival/adverbial 'straight / true,' آمدش is the past verb 'came' with the enclitic pronoun -ش = 'to him,' and گفتار او ('his speech/words') is the noun phrase whose motion is described. Literally: 'indeed, his words came straight to him.' Note the poetic word order: modifier + verb(+enclitic) + noun phrase.
segments.s167-g133: فرنگیس is the speaker's name (subject). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here used to introduce direct speech). ای is the vocative particle 'O,' خردمند is an epithet 'wise' and شاه is the vocative noun 'king' — together addressing the king as 'O wise king.'
segments.s167-g134: مکن is the prohibitive imperative 'do not.' هیچ‌گونه is an adverbial quantifier 'in no way / at all.' به ما در نگاه is a prepositional phrase with به = 'to/at,' ما = 'us,' and در نگاه = 'in (your) gaze / look' — together 'do not in any way look at us.' Word order places the negative verb before the adverbial quantifier and the object phrase.
segments.s167-g135: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'one time' / 'for even one occasion' (i.e. 'not even once' in negative context). گام‌زن is a vocative 'walker' or 'one who marches,' and برنشین is the imperative 'sit down' / 'take a seat.' Together (with the negation that follows) it carries the sense 'do not sit even once, O marcher.'
segments.s167-g136: مباش is the negative imperative form 'do not be.' ایچ is an emphatic negative 'at all / nothing,' ایمن is the adjective 'safe,' and به توران زمین is the locative expression 'in the land of Turan' (به = 'in/to,' توران = 'Turan,' زمین = 'land/country'). The clause means 'do not be at all safe in the land of Turan' — i.e. 'you are not safe at all in Turan.'
segments.s167-g209: دگر here functions as a temporal/discourse particle meaning 'then' or 'again' at the start of the clause. مرکبان را همه is the definite object phrase ('all the mounts / all the horsemen'), with را marking definiteness. کرد پی is a causative/transferive construction: کرد ('caused/made') takes پی ('in pursuit / after') as its complement, so the phrase means 'he caused/put all the horsemen to follow/pursue.'
segments.s167-g210: برافروخت is a past narrative verb 'he kindled/ignited.' The following برسان functions as a coordinated verbal element meaning 'sent/caused to reach' (a poetic/truncated form used in narrative). آتش ز نی is a source phrase 'fire from reed(s)' (i.e. torches made of reeds); ز is the poetic preposition 'from.' Altogether: 'he kindled and sent fire made of reeds.'
segments.s167-g211: خود و سرکشان is the compound subject 'he himself and his followers/companions' (سرکشان here means his men/followers). سوی ایران is a directional phrase 'toward Iran.' کشید is a past verb 'drew/moved' (here intransitive: 'turned/moved toward'). The clause means 'he and his followers turned toward Iran.'
segments.s167-g212: رخ is 'face(s)' (the grammatical subject of this short clause). از خون دیده‌شده is a modifying phrase 'seen with blood / stained with blood' (دیده‌شده is a passive/perfect participle 'seen' used adjectivally). ناپدید is the predicate/adjectival result 'vanished / became invisible.' Word order places the modifier before the final predicate: 'faces, seen with blood, disappeared.'
segments.s167-g213: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when.' یک نیم فرسنگ is the quantified distance 'one and a half farsangs.' ببرید راه is a poetic/idiomatic past phrasing meaning 'they cut/traversed the road' — i.e. 'they had gone (that distance).' The numeral + unit precedes the verb, which is typical in this kind of poetic narration.
segments.s167-g214: رسید is the past verb 'arrived / reached.' اندرو is an adverbial/locative 'there / in that place' (poetic form). شاه توران سپاه is a poetic noun phrase in inverted order meaning 'the army of the king of Turan' (سپاه = army; شاه توران = the Turanian king). So the clause means 'the Turanian king's army arrived there.'
segments.s167-g215: سپه دید is 'he saw the army/host' (سپه = army; دید = saw). با خود و تیغ و زره is an accompanying phrase introduced by با: 'with himself — sword and armor' (i.e. he had sword and armor with him or in his company). The coordination links خود, تیغ and زره as things accompanying the perceiver.
segments.s167-g216: سیاوش is the proper name 'Siavash.' زده is a past participle/predicate 'struck' (indicating having been hit). بر زره بر گره is a locative phrase 'on the armor, at the knot/buckle' (the repeated بر is poetic; گره 'knot' here refers to a fastening/buckle). Together: 'Siavash, struck, (was) on the armor at the buckle' — i.e. he bore a blow at the armor's fastening.
segments.s167-g81: سوی روم (toward Rome) is a prepositional/ directional phrase placed before the rest of the clause; ره (way/journey) is the nominal theme and با درنگ (with delay) is an adverbial phrase modifying the coming. آیدت is the verb آمدن with the enclitic -ت ‘to you’ (literally “it comes to you”), used here in the simple/predictive sense to mean the journey will be slow or will come upon you slowly. The word order (direction + noun + adverb + verb) is typical of older/poetic Persian where the verb comes late and pronouns attach to the verb.
segments.s167-g82: نپویی is a negative jussive/imperative form meaning “do not go/seek” addressed to the second person. سوی چین is the directional object ‘toward China.’ که here introduces a causal/explanatory clause (“for/because”), and تنگ آیدت uses تنگ (narrow, difficult) plus آمدن with enclitic -ت to mean ‘it will come to you as narrow’ — idiomatically ‘it will be cramped/difficult for you.’
segments.s167-g83: ز گیتی کراگیری literally ‘taking levies from the world’ uses ز + noun to mark source/target and کراگیری as a verbal-noun (‘collection, levying’). اکنون پناه places اکنون (now) before پناه (refuge) and the copula is omitted; the whole phrase is best read as an instruction or proposal: “let collecting tribute from the world be your refuge now,” with the usual poetic inversion and an omitted verb such as باشد (‘be’).
segments.s167-g84: پناهت is پناه ‘refuge’ with the enclitic -ت ‘your.’ Here it is followed appositionally by خداوند خورشید و ماه ‘the Lord of the sun and moon’ (an epithet for the divine or sovereign), with no copula; the clause functions as a benediction/wish: ‘May the Lord of sun and moon be your refuge/protection.’
segments.s167-g85: This is a classical optative/curse formula: ستم باد combines a noun (ستم, oppression) with باد used as an optative particle (‘may it be’). بر جان او ‘upon his life’ is the target of the curse, and ماه و سال (‘month and year’) is an adverbial phrase meaning continually/for years. The order (noun + باد + object + time phrase) is idiomatic in older poetry.
segments.s167-g86: کجا here is a rhetorical interrogative (‘how/when/where?’) implying disbelief; بر تن تو شود literally ‘becomes upon your body,’ where شود (شد) is the verb ‘become/happen’ and بدسگال is the predicate ‘ill‑fated/ill‑omened.’ Together it reads as a rhetorical question: ‘How could ill‑fate befall your body?’
segments.s167-g87: همی گفت here uses همی as an iterative/progressive marker (‘kept/was saying’), followed by the speaker’s name گرسیوز. اکنون ز راه is a temporal + directional phrase (‘now from the road’) placed before the verb (a common poetic word order), so the clause prepares the content that follows in the next segment.
segments.s167-g88: بیاید is the present/future form of آمدن used here as ‘(he) comes/will come.’ همانا (‘verily/indeed’) is a discourse particle emphasizing the statement. ز نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase ‘from near, (the) king,’ with شاه (king) at the end for emphasis; the overall meaning is that the king is approaching from nearby.
segments.s167-g233: A short reporting clause: چنین ('thus') comes first as a discourse adverb, then گفت (here the verb 'said') and the name گرسیوز, followed by the compound adjective کم‌خرد ('of little wisdom' = foolish). Note: گفت is the past narrative verb ('he said'); کم modifies خرد to form the adjective 'foolish'.
segments.s167-g234: A relative/interrogative clause introduced by کزین (literally 'from this / of this'); در سخن خود = 'in/with his speech' (در + noun + possessive خود). کی is the interrogative 'who' here, and اندر خورد is a verbal phrase meaning 'to take effect / to be acted on' (خورد used in sense 'befall/ happen' after اندر). The entire clause asks rhetorically who would be affected by such words.
segments.s167-g235: A conditional protasis beginning with گر ('if'). ایدر ('here') and چنین ('thus/so') qualify the conditional, بی‌گناه is the predicate adjective 'innocent', and آمدی is 2nd‑person past 'you came' — used here to state the supposition ('if you came...').
segments.s167-g236: An interrogative main clause: چرا ('why') introduces the question; با زره ('with/ wearing armor') expresses accompaniment or instrument; نزد شاه ('in the presence of the king') marks the goal/recipient; آمدی is again 2nd‑person past 'did you come'. Word order places the question word first followed by the instrumental/locative phrase and the verb at the end, typical of Persian.
segments.s167-g237: پذیره شدن is a deverbal noun + شدن forming a passive/nominal idea 'being received/acceptance'. زین نشان here means 'by/for this mark/token' (کز/زین = 'by this'), and راه نیست is an idiom 'is not the (proper) way' / 'is not permitted'. The clause is a general negative judgement about accepting someone for that reason.
segments.s167-g238: A simple nominal clause: سنان و سپر ('spear and shield') is the compound subject, هدیه شاه نیست is the negative copular phrase 'are not the king's gift' (هدیه = gift, شاه = king, نیست = is not). The copula is explicit and negated.
segments.s167-g239: A reporting clause: سیاوش (name) + بدانست ('he knew/realized it') — بدانست is the past tense verb meaning 'he perceived/understood'. کان (an archaic demonstrative/conjunction here taken as 'that/this') introduces the content clause; کار اوست = 'it is his affair/plan' with اوست the copula 'is (his)'.
segments.s167-g240: This clause completes what 'the plan' is: برآشفتن (averbal infinitive/noun) 'to anger / to upset', followed by شه ('the king') as its object. ز introduces the reason/ground ('because of' / 'from'), بازار 'the bazaar/market', and اوست is the copula tying this predicate back to 'کار' (i.e. 'the plan is to anger the king because of the bazaar').
segments.s167-g441: سیاوش is the subject (proper name). بنالید is a past, poetic 3rd-person singular form meaning “moaned/wailed” (the verbal prefix بـ is integrated into the past form). با + کردگار here expresses the addressee/recipient of the lament — classical usage where با functions like “to/with” when addressing God; کردگار is the addressee ‘the Creator/God.’ Word order is Subject + Verb + (prepositional phrase marking the addressee).
segments.s167-g442: که‌ای should be read as که + ای (a relative/vocative clause: “O you who …/who are …”). برتر از is a comparative construction: برتر (superior/beyond) + از (than/from). گردش روزگار is a genitive noun phrase ‘the turning/cycle of time (fate)’ — گردش (turning, course) of روزگار (time/fate). Together the clause addresses the Creator as one above the vicissitudes of time.
segments.s167-g443: یکی is an indefinite numeral here (“one/a certain”). شاخ is used metaphorically as a ‘branch/shoot’ (offspring line). پیدا کن is an imperative: پیدا (appear/found) + کن (do!) — together meaning “bring forth / cause to appear.” از تخم من is a prepositional phrase “from my seed/origin” marking source; من functions possessively here (‘my’). The whole is an imperative request to produce a descendant.
segments.s167-g444: چو is a comparative/simile conjunction meaning “like/as.” خورشید تابنده is a noun plus participle pattern: خورشید (sun) + تابنده (shining/resplendent) as an attributive. بر انجمن is a prepositional phrase “upon/over the assembly (court/council).” The phrase compares the hoped-for branch to the sun shining over the assembly.
segments.s167-g445: که introduces a relative clause “who …”. خواهد is a modal/future-like verb here (3rd-person) meaning “will/wishes” and governs the following clause. ازین (این in classical spelling) + دشمنان = “from/against these enemies.” کین خویش is the noun + possessive split across خوی + ش (classical orthography): کین = hatred/vengeance, and خوی + ش = his own (possessive), so together “his hatred.”
segments.s167-g446: کند is the 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used after خواهد to complete the relative clause: خواهد … کند (“who will … do / who will cause …”). تازه functions as an object/adverb meaning “renew/make fresh.” در کشور = “in the country/realm,” and آیین خویش (again split as خوی + ش) = “his rites/customs.” The clause means to renew his rites and make his enmity active again in his land.
segments.s167-g447: همی شد is a poetic past/imperfect pattern (همی + شد) indicating a resulting state “then/became.” پس is temporal/adverbial “then/after.” پشت او is “his back.” پیلسم is an archaic/obscure noun (textual/lexical uncertainty in classical Persian); here it names the condition/state that his back took — the exact meaning is uncertain in modern Persian but treated as a noun in predicate position.
segments.s167-g448: دیده (here preceded by دو in the surface text) = “eye(s).” پر + از = a fixed adjectival phrase meaning “full of” (پر از خون = “full of blood”). و joins clauses. دل پر ز غم uses the poetic variant ز (for از) after پر to mean “full with/of sorrow”: دل (heart) + پر ز غم = “heart full of grief.” Word order is noun + predicate adjective phrase in both coordinated parts.
segments.s167-g169: This is a prepositional range phrase built with the poetic preposition ز ... تا: ز + noun+adjective + تا + noun. خورشید تابنده is noun (خورشید) followed by a postpositive adjective (تابنده). تا introduces the limit 'as far as/to the dark earth' (تیره‌خاک). The whole phrase means 'from the shining sun to the dark earth.'
segments.s167-g170: گذر نیست is an impersonal negative: 'there is no passage/escape.' از introduces the source/thing one cannot escape. داد یزدان پاک is a noun phrase 'the justice of the pure God' (داد = 'justice', یزدان = 'God', پاک = 'pure' adjectival). Together: 'there is no escape from the justice of the pure God.'
segments.s167-g171: به خواری is an adverbial phrase 'into/with humiliation.' ترا is the 2nd-person object (ت) plus the object marker را; روزبانان شاه is the subject 'the king's heralds' (روزبانان modified by شاه). Word order places the adverbial and object before the subject for emphasis: 'to humiliation you, the king's heralds...'.
segments.s167-g172: سر و تن is a coordinated noun phrase 'head and body' (objects placed before the verb as is common in Persian). برهنه is a postpositive adjective modifying both. برندت is a third-person plural verb meaning 'they lead/take' with the enclitic -ت marking 'you' (object). به راه is an adverbial 'on/along the road.' The clause therefore says they will lead you, head and body naked, along the road.
segments.s167-g173: بیاید is used as an optative/future-like verb 'may/shall come' (poetic ordering places the verb before subject). سپهدار پیران is the compound subject 'the commander of the elders.' به در is a locative set phrase 'to the door/gate,' here meaning 'to the presence/court.'
segments.s167-g174: بخواهش functions here as 'by/requesting' (literally 'by petition' or 'with a request'); بخواهد is a third-person singular present/future 'he will ask/claim' (desire/request). ترا is the object 'you' (clitic) and از پدر marks 'from the father' as the source. Together: 'and (he) will, by petition, demand you from your father.'
segments.s167-g175: به جان بی‌گنه is an oath-like phrase 'by the life/soul of the innocent' (به + noun + adjective). خواهدت is third-person singular future with the enclitic -ت 'you' (object). زینهار here means 'a warning' or 'admonition' (object of خواهدت): 'he will give you a warning' or 'he will admonish you.'
segments.s167-g176: به ایوان خویشش is a destination phrase 'to his own iwan/porch/hall' (خویشش = 'his own', possessive). برد here functions verbally 'he will lead/take (you)' and the adjectives زار و خوار follow as predicative adjectives describing the object ('wretched and despised'). In short: 'he will bring you to his hall, wretched and despised.' (Note: برد in the literal wordlist appears as a noun sense elsewhere; here it is used as a verb 'to take/lead'.)
segments.s167-g385: بیازرد is an optative/jussive-like verb here expressing a wish (’may he not harm / may he not be harmed’); it governs شاه را, which is the definite object marked by the postposition را. از بهر تو is a prepositional phrase ('for/on account of you') placed before the object for emphasis in classical style.
segments.s167-g386: چنان is a demonstrative/adverb ('such' / 'so') modifying the following list of nouns. افسر، تخت، آن گاه are coordinated nouns ('helmet, throne, that time/occasion'); the final را is the definite/object/topic marker applied to the whole coordinated phrase (a classical device to mark emphasis or a second object).
segments.s167-g387: بیامد is a simple past verb 'came'. ترا is the clitic object 'you' (ت + را) and is the object of کرد; کرد is the main verb 'made/caused', and پشت و پناه are the predicate nouns meaning 'support and refuge' (i.e. 'he made you [into] a support and refuge'). Classical Persian often strings verbs and clitics in this terse order.
segments.s167-g388: کنون is an adverb 'now'. زو is the contracted classical form of از + او meaning 'from him' or 'in/from him' (expressing the source). چه دیدی is a past interrogative 'what did you see' (دیدی = you saw); که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/because' and governs بردت ز راه, where بردت here would mean 'took/carried you' (led you away) and ز راه = 'from the road/path'. Note: the listed sense options for بردت do not include the common verb sense 'to carry/lead', so that sense is marked as unavailable below.
segments.s167-g389: نبرد is the noun 'battle/contest'. سر تاجداران is a genitive-type phrase 'the head/top of the crown-bearers' (i.e. of the crowned/sovereigns). کسی here is 'someone' or 'anyone' and begins the relative idea continued in the next segment; the line uses terse, classical ordering where the general statement is given and the qualifying relative clause follows.
segments.s167-g390: که is the relative/conjunction 'who/that'. با + تاج بر تخت ماند is a compact clause 'who with the crown upon the throne remains' (ماند = remain, endure). بسی is an adverb here (classical use) indicating extent ('much/long' or emphatic 'indeed/often'); the phrase describes someone who stays on the throne wearing the crown. Word order is classical: relative clause follows the head noun.
segments.s167-g391: مکن is the prohibitive imperative 'do not'. بی‌گنه is an adjective 'innocent' modifying 'تن من' ('my body' or 'my person'); this prepositional phrase بر تن من ('upon my body') marks the target of ستم. ستم is the noun 'oppression/injustice'. Together this gives a negative command: 'Do not oppress my innocent body.'
segments.s167-g392: که is causal 'for/because'. گیتی سپنج است is a nominal clause 'the world is a sponge' (سپنج = sponge, used metaphorically). با باد و دم 'with wind and breath' qualifies how the world is like a sponge (i.e. easily soaked/affected by breath/wind). است is the copula 'is'. Altogether: 'for the world is a sponge to wind and breath.'
segments.s167-g329: بدو is the prepositional/clitic phrase 'to him' (literally 'to him/unto it') placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (here the dataset's senses don't list the verbal sense); گرسیوز‌ای is the vocative/name with ای introducing an address ('Garsivaz, O ...'); هوشمند is a descriptive vocative 'wise/clever'. Together the segment sets up who speaks to whom and the vocative address.
segments.s167-g330: بگفت is an alternative/poetic past 'he said'; the following clause is an imperative/negative admonition. جوانان functions as a plural attributive ('the young/young men') modifying هوا (the desires/whims of youth); را is the definite/object marker attached to هوا; مبند is the negative imperative 'do not bind/attach' (i.e. 'do not be bound by / do not heed'). The order is Verb (said) → object clause (do not ...).
segments.s167-g331: از ایرانیان indicates origin or 'among (the) Iranians' (از = 'from/among'); دشت is the subject 'the plain'; پر + کرگس is a predicative phrase 'full of vultures' (پر = 'full (of)', followed by the thing it is full of); است is the copula 'is'. The sequence is subject → predicate → copula, typical of Persian poetic order.
segments.s167-g332: گر is the conditional 'if'; از کین بترسی is 'if you fear (from) vengeance' where بترسی is present/subjunctive used in conditional sense 'you fear'; the enclitic ت (appearing as ترا when combined) marks the second person affected ('for you'); این بس است literally 'this is enough' — with ترا (for you) placed before the predicate for emphasis: 'this is enough for you.'
segments.s167-g333: همین بد 'this very bad/evil' is the head phrase; که کردی is a relative clause 'that you did' with کردی as the past-tense verb 'you did' (the separate ت is the 2nd-person clitic as given in the word list); را here appears as the object marker attached in the line's morphology; خود 'yourself' emphasizes the beneficiary; بس 'enough' is the predicate (the negative 'نه' in the line negates it — the clause means 'is not enough for you'). Word order is demonstrative/adjective → head → relative clause → dative/emphatic 'for you' → predicate.
segments.s167-g334: که here links and continues the thought ('and/so that'); خیره 'staring/astonished' describes the mental state; همی is the habitual/continuous particle 'still/ever'; بشنوی is present-subjunctive/imperative form 'you hear / you listen' used here descriptively; پند کس 'the advice of anyone' (پند = advice, کس = any person). The clause says he remains amazed and keeps hearkening to anyone's counsel.
segments.s167-g335: سیاوش is the subject/name; چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; بخروشد is a poetic verb meaning 'rushes/roars/charges' (here third-person), so 'when Siavash charges forth'; از روم و چین indicates origin/directions 'from Rome and China' (i.e. from the west and the east).
segments.s167-g336: پر از ... is the predicate 'full of ...' (پر = 'full (of)', followed by از + list 'گرز و شمشیر' = 'maces and swords'); بینی is the 2nd-person singular present 'you see' (so the clause literally reads 'full of maces and swords you see the earth'); زمین is the object 'the earth/ground' — poetic word order places the predicative phrase before the verb and object for effect.
segments.s167-g417: همی is a poetic particle marking ongoing or imminent action (here giving a future/continuous sense). شهریاری is the noun 'sovereignty/kingship' placed before the verb for emphasis. ربایی is the verb (2nd‑person singular by implication) 'you seize/you will seize'. ز گاه is a prepositional phrase 'from the time/at the hour' (ز = poetic 'from', گاه = 'time/appointed hour') modifying the verb.
segments.s167-g418: درین = در + این (fused) means 'in this', so درین کار = 'in this matter/affair' (در = 'in', ین = variant of 'این'). به زین نگه کن is an imperative: به + زین = 'onto/at the saddle', نگه = 'keeping/watch', کن = imperative 'do/keep' → 'keep watch on the saddle'. پگاه is an adverbial time 'at dawn/early morning'.
segments.s167-g419: مده is the negative imperative 'do not give/hand over'. شهر توران = 'the city of Turan' (شهر = city, توران = Turan). به خیره is a prepositional phrase 'into/with astonishment/stunnedness' (به = 'to/into', خیره = 'stunned/staring'). به باد is idiomatic 'to the wind' (به = 'to', باد = 'wind') meaning 'to be lost/ruined'.
segments.s167-g420: بباید که is an impersonal/modal construction 'it is necessary / it must be that' (introducing a clause). روز بد 'a bad day' (روز = day, بد = bad) is the subject/theme of the subordinate clause. آیدت is the verb 'comes to you / befalls you' (آید + enclitic ت 'to you'), and یاد here is a noun 'remembrance/memory' used so the clause means 'a bad day will come to your remembrance' (= 'you will have cause to remember a bad day').
segments.s167-g421: بگفت is past tense 'he said'. این و = 'this and/then' (connecting the speech to the following action). روی سیاوش بدید is a nominal phrase + past verb: روی = 'face', سیاوش = proper name, بدید = past 'saw' → 'he saw the face of Siyâvash'. Word order is typical classical Persian: predicate (بگفت) then coordinated clause with verb at the end.
segments.s167-g422: دو رخ را = 'his two cheeks/faces' with را marking the definite direct object. بکند is a past tense verb here meaning an action performed on those cheeks (in context: tearing, wrenching, or violently moving them in grief — classical poetic usage allows such verbs). و connects to فغان برکشید: فغان = 'lament/cry', برکشید = past 'raised/lifted' → 'and he raised a cry'.
segments.s167-g423: دل شاه توران = 'the heart of the king of Turan' (possessive chain). برو here functions archaically as a dative/benefactive-like element 'for him / toward him' (an old/poetic contraction not represented by the simple lexical senses listed), and بر بسوخت is the verb phrase: بر + بسوخت (passive/past 'was burned'/'burned upon') → 'burned for/against him' i.e. the king's heart burned because of him. (If read more idiomatically: 'the Turanian king's heart was consumed with grief/anger at him').
segments.s167-g424: همی again gives a continuous/emphatic sense. خیره modifies 'چشم' as 'fixed/staring'; چشم خرد را = 'the eye of wisdom' (چشم = eye, خرد = wisdom) with را marking the object. بدوخت is past of دوختن 'to sew/close/fasten' (here metaphorical: 'he closed/sewed up the eye of wisdom' with his fixed stare), so the verb completes the action on the object.
segments.s167-g361: و لیکن is a coordinating concession (‚and yet/ but'); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; گفت here functions as a noun 'saying/word' (the utterance); ستاره شمر is a genitive phrase 'the star Shamr' (a named star). Word order is literary: 'ز گفت ستاره شمر' = 'from the saying of the star Shamr.'
segments.s167-g362: به فرجام = 'in/at the end' (فرجام = outcome/end). ز + و here form z + pronoun (زو) meaning 'from him' (the listed و appears as the enclitic pronoun rather than the conjunction). سختی آید به سر is an idiom: آید (present/habitual/perfective sense 'comes'/'befalls') + به سر 'to the head' = 'hardship will befall him.' The verb آید is classical/present but carries the sense 'will happen/has happened' in context.
segments.s167-g363: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/colloquial particle meaning roughly 'now/at this moment' or 'hereupon'. خونش = خون + possessive -ش 'his' ('his blood'). بریزم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive/optative used for the conditional 'I spill/shed'; به کین = 'for vengeance'. Overall: 'If I now shed his blood for vengeance...'.
segments.s167-g364: یکی is an indefinite numeral 'one / a certain' (here 'a single' or 'a certain one'). گرد خیزد = 'a dust/host rises' (گرد here = dust/commotion, خیزد = 'rises/appears' in present/future sense). ز ایران‌زمین = 'from the land of Iran' (ز = 'from'). The clause predicts that a force/commotion will arise from Iran.
segments.s167-g365: رها کردنش is the infinitive noun phrase 'to let him go / his release' (رها + کردن as a light verb + enclitic -ش 'him'). بتر از is a comparative construction (classical 'بتر' here functions as 'better' / 'preferable') followed by از + noun; کشتنست = کشتن + است (archaic contraction -ست) 'is killing'. So: 'Letting him go is better than killing (him).' (Note: کردن is used as auxiliary/ light verb.)
segments.s167-g366: همان = 'that very / the same'; کشتنش = the gerund/nominalized verb 'his killing' (کشتن + -ش). رنج و درد = a fixed pair 'suffering and pain' (both nouns in apposition). منست is an archaic copular/possessive construction meaning 'is mine' or 'pertains to me' (here: 'that killing is my sorrow'). The clause states that the killing would be the speaker's grief.
segments.s167-g367: به توران here marks the source/relatum 'from Turan' (classical phrasing can use به to indicate the party causing the harm). گزند = 'harm/offense'. مرا is the object 'to me' (in the manuscript split as م + را); آمدست = آمد + ست (archaic perfect/copula) 'has come / has befallen'. So syntactically: 'Harm from Turan has befallen me.'
segments.s167-g368: A loose apposition listing the afflictions: غم و درد و بند = 'sorrow and pain and bondage/fetter'. Again مرا آمدست ('has come to me / has befallen me') repeats the perfective copula. بند here is physical or figurative 'bond/captivity'. The verb آمدست is classical and indicates that these states have befallen the speaker.
segments.s167-g217: به دل گفت (literal 'said to the heart') is an idiom meaning 'he said to himself / he thought'; گفت is past-tense 'said'. The sequence 'این راست گفت' places the demonstrative 'این' + adjective 'راست' before the verb گفت: 'he said, "this is true."
segments.s167-g218: سخن is the noun 'a saying / words'; زین here is the contracted/poetic form equivalent to 'از این' ('from this' / 'on this sign'); نشانی 'sign' takes a following relative clause. The clause 'که بد در نهفت' is a relative clause ('that...') with نهفت (archaic perfect/past participle of نهفتن) meaning 'was hidden' and بد qualifying what lay hidden.
segments.s167-g219: This is a simple past/clausal phrase: سیاوش is the subject and بترسید is the past-tense verb 'became afraid / feared'. The prepositional phrase از بیم جان ('from/because of fear for his life') gives the cause/ground of his fear.
segments.s167-g220: مگر here functions adverbially as 'perhaps / maybe' (a classical usage). گفت is 'he said/thought', introducing the content clause بدخواه گردد نهان. گردد is present-subjunctive/optative (meaning 'may become' / 'has become' depending on context) expressing possibility; نهان is a predicative adjective/adverb meaning 'hidden' or 'in hiding'.
segments.s167-g221: همی is a habitual/progressive particle giving an imperfective sense ('kept / was continually'). بنگرید is a narrative form meaning 'he looked' or 'he kept looking'. The elliptic sequence 'این بدان آن بدین' uses the preposition/prefix ب- + demonstratives to mean 'from this to that, and from that to this' (a classical, parallel expression meaning 'this way and that').
segments.s167-g222: که introduces an explanatory/subordinate clause. کینه نبدشان: نبد (negative existential/preterite) + شان (their) = 'there was not (for them) hatred'. به دل marks the locus 'in the heart'. پیش ازین is a temporal phrase 'before this / previously'. Overall: 'for there had been no grudge in their hearts before.'
segments.s167-g223: ز (poetic 'از') + بیم سیاوش gives the causal phrase 'because of / from fear for Siyavash'. سواران جنگ is the nominal phrase 'the horsemen of war' (i.e., the fighting riders) serving as subject for the following verb.
segments.s167-g224: گرفتند is past plural 'they took / they seized / they adopted'. The three nouns آرام و هوش و درنگ are the coordinated objects/qualities they 'took' or 'assumed'—'calm, alertness/awareness, and restraint/pausing'—an idiomatic way to say they became calm, alert, and restrained.
segments.s167-g9: مرا (م + را) is the object 'me' with the postposition/marker را attached for definiteness/emphasis; خواستی is 2nd-person past/present-past 'you wanted/sought' (خواستن). شاد گشتم is a perfective past of گشتن used as 'I became happy' (شاد = happy, گشتم = I became). بد‌ان is an imperative information verb 'know' formed from the prefix ب- + دان; here it introduces the following clause.
segments.s167-g10: که is the conjunction 'that' introducing a subordinate clause. بادا is an optative particle 'may it be' (wishing or blessing). نشست here is a nominal/abstract use 'sitting/seat/session' (your sitting/seat), تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you', با is the preposition 'with', and موبدان (plural of موبد) names the Zoroastrian priests — together: 'may your seat be with the mobeds.'
segments.s167-g11: و 'and' links this clause to the previous; دیگر here means 'again' / 'another' (marking repetition or a second case). فرنگیس را is the proper name Farangis with the object marker را. خواستی is again 2nd-person past 'you desired' (خواستن).
segments.s167-g12: به مهر و وفا is an adverbial phrase 'with love and fidelity' introduced by به (here functioning like 'with/by'); مهر = love, وفا = fidelity. دل is the direct object 'heart' (the thing being adorned), and بیاراستی is the 2nd-person past form of the archaic verb بیاراستن 'to adorn/make beautiful' — 'you adorned (your) heart.'
segments.s167-g13: فرنگیس is the subject (the name Farangis). نالنده is a participial adjective/noun meaning 'lamenting'/'one who laments' used predicatively. بود here functions as the past auxiliary 'was' (auxiliary to form past continuous/state). این زمان = 'at this time' (این = this, زمان = time/period).
segments.s167-g14: به لب ناچران is a prepositional phrase 'on/at (her) lips, compelled' (به + لب; ناچران = compelled/forced), and به تن ناچمان parallels it 'and in/with the body distressed' (تن = body; ناچمان = unwilling/distressed). The pair describes her outward speech (forced on the lips) and inner state (distressed in the body).
segments.s167-g15: بخفت is the simple past 'he slept' (archaic/poetic past of خوابیدن). و 'and' connects clauses. مرا (m + را) is 'me' with the object marker را. پیش بالین = 'before/at the pillow' (پیش = before/in the presence of, بالین = pillow/bedside). ببست is a past form of بستن ('he bound/fastened/closed/placed'); here it means he placed or made me fast/laid me by the pillow.
segments.s167-g16: میان دو گیتیش = 'between the two worlds' (میان = between, دو = two, گیتیش = 'گیتی' with a possessive/relational ending here meaning 'worlds/realms'). بینم نشست literally 'he sat in my sight' (بینم = in my sight / before me, نشست = he sat/took a seat) — together: 'he took a seat between the two worlds, before my eyes.'
segments.s167-g33: This clause opens with the prepositional/clitic phrase 'بدو' (to him) followed by the past verb 'گفت' (said). 'چون' here functions temporally/causal ('when' / 'since'), introducing the subordinate clause. 'تیره شد' is a compound of adjective + past verb meaning 'became dark/ominous', and 'روی کار' is a noun phrase in the order 'face (روی) + affair (کار)' = 'the face/aspect of affairs'.
segments.s167-g34: 'نشاید' is an impersonal/archaic present-stative meaning 'it is not fitting/it must not'; it governs the following infinitive 'شمردن' ('to reckon/consider'). The preposition 'به' here is best understood as 'by/according to', followed by 'بد روزگار' ('ill/unfavorable times') with 'بد' modifying 'روزگار'.
segments.s167-g35: This simple clause has the proper name 'سیاوش' as subject and the negated past verb 'نکرد' ('did not do'). 'ایچ' is an emphatic negative 'nothing/at all', and 'بر کس نگاه' means 'a look/gaze at anyone' with 'بر' expressing the target ('at/on') and 'کس' 'anyone'. The whole yields 'Siavash did not cast even a glance at anyone.'
segments.s167-g36: This clause states that no reception/welcome occurred for the speaker. 'پذیره' is a noun 'reception/acceptance', 'نیامد' is the negative past 'did not come', and the object pronoun is split as 'م' + 'را' (here intended together as 'مرا' = 'me'); 'خود' is an emphatic reflexive 'myself', and 'به راه' is an adverbial phrase 'on the road / in the journey'.
segments.s167-g37: 'سخن نیز نشنید' uses 'نیز' ('also') with the negative past verb 'نشنید' to say 'he did not hear speech either.' The coordinating 'و' links the second negative clause 'نامه نخواند' — 'he did not read the letter.' Word order is Subject (implied he) + object + verb in both coordinated negative clauses.
segments.s167-g38: This clause begins with the object clitic split as 'م' + 'را' (= 'مرا', 'me'), followed by the locative phrase 'پیش تختش' ('before/at his throne') and the prepositional phrase 'به زانو' ('on the knee'). The verb 'نشاند' is an archaic/determinative past transitive meaning 'caused to sit/placed'. The whole means 'he set me before his throne on his knee.'
segments.s167-g39: 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' meaning 'from', so 'ز ایران' = 'from Iran'. 'بدو' is the dative 'to him', 'نامه' is the noun 'letter', and 'پیوسته شد' is a light verbal phrase meaning 'continued/kept happening' or 'was continually' (i.e., letters kept arriving). Word order is source + dative + theme + verbal predicate.
segments.s167-g40: 'به مادر' is a prepositional phrase 'toward/for his mother', 'همی' is an archaic continuous/adverbial particle ('still/continually'), and 'مهر او' 'his/her affection' is the possessed noun phrase. 'بسته شد' is a passive/resultative pattern meaning 'became bound/fastened' or more naturally 'became fixed/firm' — together: 'his love for his mother remained/became firmly fixed.'
segments.s167-g281: چرا is the interrogative adverb 'why' placed at the start. کشت is used as the notion of 'killing' (a verbal noun/root) combined with خواهی (second-person present/future 'you will/wish') to express the agent's intention; کسی را is the definite object marked by را (object/emphasis) and که introduces a relative clause modifying 'someone' ('who ...'). تاج begins that relative clause ('who has a crown').
segments.s167-g282: بگرید is a narrative/optative-form verb meaning 'let him/her/it weep' or 'he weeps' in a poetic register; بُرو is an imperative 'go'; زار here qualifies the action as 'mournful/wretched' (an adverbial/epithet); با introduces an accompaniment or locus 'with/at', followed by تخت عاج 'the ivory throne' (تخت = throne, عاج = ivory). The whole clause is a contemptuous/imperative comment: 'go and lament at the ivory throne.'
segments.s167-g283: سری را places 'head' as a topical/definite phrase with را (emphatic/object-marker). کجا is the interrogative 'where/whither' and تاج باشد is a predicative clause ('a crown be/should be'), with باشد expressing possibility/existence in a poetic register. کلاه follows as a nominative/contrastive noun ('the cap/hat') tied to the next clause.
segments.s167-g284: نشاید is an impersonal modal/ethical verb meaning 'it is not fitting/one should not.' برید here functions as 'take/remove' (classical usage of بردن) so کلاه نشاید برید = 'one should not remove the cap.' ای خردمند شاه is a vocative phrase 'O wise king' addressing the addressee.
segments.s167-g285: به هنگام شادی is a temporal prepositional phrase 'at the time of joy' (به + هنگام + noun). درختی is an indefinite noun 'a tree' (the suffix -ی marks indefiniteness or adjectival sense) followed by the adjective مکار 'deceitful/treacherous' after the noun, as is common in Persian.
segments.s167-g286: که is a relative conjunction 'that/which' introducing the explanation clause. زهر is 'poison'; آورد is the verb 'brings/produces' (narrative/present sense); بار here means 'burden/fruit/load' and is qualified by او 'its' (referring back to the tree), while روزگار ('days/fate') indicates the domain affected — 'its fruit brings poison to one's days.'
segments.s167-g287: همی is a continuous/adverbial particle 'still/always,' and بود is the past copula 'was.' گرسیوز is the proper name 'Garsivaz' functioning as the subject; بدنشان ('their bodies' / 'their persons') follows and here indicates closeness or presence among them — a poetic way to say 'he was continually among them in person.' Word order (verb before subject) is typical of classical style.
segments.s167-g288: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of.' بیهودگی is an abstract noun 'futility/meaninglessness' giving the reason. یار مردم کشان is a compound: یار 'companion' followed by مردم 'people' and کشان (present-participial/agentive suffix) '‑pulling/‑dragging'; together it evokes 'companions/people‑draggers' or those who lead people away — a descriptive noun phrase governed by ز بیهودگی (i.e., 'because of the futility of such companion‑dragging').
segments.s167-g465: ز here is the archaic/poetic preposition meaning 'of/from' used to mark possession ('of Garsivaz'); آن is a demonstrative 'that/the'; خنجر is the noun 'dagger' and آبگون is a post‑nominal adjective 'bluish'. Word order reflects older poetic Persian: preposition + possessor, then demonstrative + noun + adjective.
segments.s167-g466: گروی is used here in the sense 'join/attach' (an older/poetic verbal sense) and functions with زره as the target ('to/onto the armor'); بستد is the past tense 3rd‑person 'tied/fastened'. از بهر is a two‑word idiom 'for the sake of/for', and خون is the noun 'blood'. The clause thus says he fastened/attached (the dagger) to the armor for blood.
segments.s167-g467: بیفگند is a past preterite 'he flung/ threw away'; پیل ژیان را is the direct object with را marking definiteness — 'the living elephant' (پیل modified by ژیان, 'living'/'alive'). به خاک is the destination phrase 'to the ground/onto the earth'.
segments.s167-g468: نه ... نه ... frames a 'neither ... nor' construction. آمدش is literally 'came to him' and functions idiomatically as 'he felt/experienced' (i.e. 'shame came to him' = 'he felt shame'). زان is the demonstrative 'that' (referring back to the person), سپهبد is the title 'spahbed', and باک is 'fear'. The whole clause: 'That spahbed felt neither shame nor fear.'
segments.s167-g469: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain', تشت is 'basin', and بنهاد is the past 'he placed/put down'. زرین is an adjective 'golden'. برش here is the older compound of بر + ـش (clitic) meaning 'on him/over it' (poetic contraction of بر + +ش), so the phrase means 'he placed a golden basin upon him/it.'
segments.s167-g470: جدا کرد is a two‑word verbal phrase 'he severed/cut off' (جدا as 'apart' + کرد 'did/made'). زان is 'that', and سرو سیمین is a poetic descriptive phrase 'cypress‑(like) silver' (سرو as the cypress/metaphor for a tall beautiful head, سیمین = 'silvery'). سرش is 'his head' (سر + pronominal suffix ـش). The whole clause: 'he cut off that silver, cypress‑like head of his.'
segments.s167-g471: بجایی is an adverbial phrase 'to/at the place' and is followed by a relative clause introduced by که 'where/that'. فرموده is a past/honorific participle 'had commanded/ordered' (here 'he had ordered/appointed'), بد is an adjective/adverb 'evil/ill' or poetically qualifying (here forming part of the phrase referring to the 'blood basin' ordered), تشت is 'basin' and خون is 'blood'. Together: 'at the place which he had ordered as the blood‑basin.'
segments.s167-g472: This clause has a compact, poetic word order: گروی here is used with the sense 'join/attach/seize' (an older verbal sense) with زره 'armor' as its object; برد is intended as a past form 'took' (but the provided sense list does not show that sense, so the mapping is marked accordingly); و is the conjunction 'and'; کردش is کرد + ـش 'he made/did it' where the clitic ـش = 'it/him'; نگون is an adjective 'overturned/overthrown'. The clause therefore means 'he took (the armor's) ... and overturned it.'
segments.s167-g161: ببرند is a 3rd-person plural verb form (present/subjunctive/optative in classical/poetic Persian) meaning 'they take/carry' and here functions as a future/irrealis action. The clause contains two prepositional phrases introduced by بر: بر بیگنه ('to/onto a stranger/foreigner') marks the recipient/direction, and بر سرم ('upon my head') marks the location; the possessive suffix -م on سر (سرم) indicates 'my head'. Word order (verb then prepositional phrases) is typical of poetic inversion.
segments.s167-g162: ز is the preposition 'from' (ablative/material) and introduces خون جگر ('the blood of the jigar/heart' — a poetic expression meaning the innermost blood or emotional/heart's blood). برنهند is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form meaning 'they place upon' (literally 'they put on'), and افسرم (افسر + ـم) is the possessed noun 'my helmet' (ـم = my). The prepositional phrase ز خون جگر specifies the material/cause associated with the placing (i.e. the helmet stained/with the blood of the heart).
segments.s167-g163: نه ... نه sets up a correlative negative ('neither ... nor'). تابوت یابم is literally 'I find a coffin' where یابم is 1st-person singular present/subjunctive/optative of یابیدن ('to find/obtain') used here as a futurate/irrealis 'I will find' and negated by نه. The second coordinated noun phrase گور و کفن ('grave and shroud') is linked by و ('and'). The object/taboot appears before the verb (typical Persian order can place object before verb in poetry).
segments.s167-g164: نه introduces another negative clause. بر من ('over/on me') uses the preposition بر plus the pronoun من (me) to mark the person affected. بگرید is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive/jussive of گریستن ('to weep/lament') and here with نه = 'will not weep'. کسی ز انجمن ('anyone/from the assembly') identifies the agent (کسی here 'anyone' in the negative context) and ز انجمن ('from the assembly') specifies that group; overall: 'no one from the assembly will weep over me.'
segments.s167-g165: نهالی ('a young sapling') is the noun; مرا here represents the clitic pronoun + object marker (مرا = my / of me) attached after the noun in classical usage, yielding 'a sapling of mine' or 'I was a sapling'. خاک توران ('the soil of Turan') is a genitive noun phrase (خاک + توران) serving as the predicate complement, and بود is the past copula 'was'. The clause therefore links the sapling (possession) to the land (i.e. 'a sapling of mine was in the soil of Turan' / 'my sapling's soil was Turan').
segments.s167-g166: سرای کهن is a noun phrase 'the ancient/old dwelling' (کهن as postpositive adjective 'old/ancient' modifying سرای). کام شیران is another nominal phrase where کام ('satisfaction/fulfillment/delight') takes شیران ('lions' or by metaphor 'brave ones') in the genitive; بود is again the past copula 'was'. Together: 'the old dwelling was the delight/fulfillment of the lions.' Note: سرای here is 'dwelling/house' (poetic vocabulary).
segments.s167-g167: برین گونه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in this way/thus' (بر + این (here orthographically برین) + گونه 'manner'). خواهد + گذشتن is the future construction: خواهد (auxiliary, 'will') plus the infinitive گذشتن ('to pass/go by'), and سپهر ('the sky/heavens' = fate/circumstance in poetic usage) names what will pass. Word order in poetry places the adverbial ahead of the verbal phrase for emphasis.
segments.s167-g168: نخواهد شدن is the negative future ('will not become' or 'will not be'); here شدن is the infinitive 'to become' governed by the auxiliaryخواهد with negation. رام is an adjectival complement meaning 'tame/gentle' that pairs with شدن ('to become tame'). با من ('toward/with me') indicates the beneficiary or direction ('toward me'), and به مهر ('by/with affection') is an instrumental phrase specifying how/with what (meekness by kindness). Overall: 'it (the sky/fate) will not be made tame/gentle toward me by affection.'
segments.s167-g153: درخت (subject) = 'tree' (here metaphorically 'line' or literal tree). تو‌گر is the fused conditional 'تو گر' = 'if' (classical/poetic ordering places it after the possessor). نر specifies 'a male'. به بار آورد is an idiom 'to bear fruit / produce' (به + بار + آورد), with آورد here meaning 'bring/produce' resulting from the conditional. Word order is inverted for poetic rhythm: possessor/subject + conditional + result verb phrase.
segments.s167-g154: یکی = 'one' / 'a certain' introducing the result; نامور modifies شهریار = 'a famed/renowned king'. آورد repeats the verb 'bring/produce' and here expresses the predicted outcome of the conditional (a future/resultive meaning in poetic narrative). The phrase means 'a certain renowned king will be produced.'
segments.s167-g155: سرافراز is the name/epithet to be given ('Sarafraz' = 'honoured/lofty'). کیخسروش is the proper name Kiakhusrow (here the patient/recipient of the naming). نام کن is an imperative: 'name (him)'. In Persian the noun of the new name precedes the verb 'کن' in this construction.
segments.s167-g156: به غم خوردن او is a causal/infinitival phrase: به + غم + خوردن (خوردن here idiomatically means 'to suffer/receive' rather than literal 'eat'), with او as the affected person ('him'). دل آرام کن is an imperative clause 'make (his) heart calm' (دل = heart, آرام + کن = 'calm!'). The whole clause gives the goal/result: soothe his heart so that he does not suffer grief.
segments.s167-g157: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the verb. گردد is the subjunctive/present-form of 'become' (poetic register: 'shall become' / 'will be so'). این گنبد تیزرو is the noun phrase 'this swift/quick dome' (تیزرو an epithet), placed after the verb for style. The clause states a resulting condition.
segments.s167-g158: سرای کهن را is the definite/direct-object/topic-marked noun phrase 'the ancient house'. نخوانند is a negative present/subjunctive form of خواندن used in the sense 'to call/name' (poetic usage), here 'they will not call'. نو = 'new'. The line means that people will cease to call the old house 'new' (i.e. it will truly be old).
segments.s167-g159: ازین پس = از این پس 'from this point forward / henceforth' (از + این + پس; here written as ازین پس). به فرمان افراسیاب = 'by the command of Afrasiab' (به used in the instrumental/agency sense 'by' + فرمان 'command' + proper name). This phrase sets time and agency.
segments.s167-g160: مرا ... را is the object/experiencer marker ('مَرا' = 'to me', here segmented into م + را in the word list). تیره‌بخت = 'ill‑fated / misfortune' (used as the thing that befalls). اندرآید is a verbal-form meaning 'comes upon / befalls' (subjunctive/poetic). به خواب = 'in a dream' (خواب here = dream). The whole clause says that misfortune will befall me in a dream / during sleep.
segments.s167-g201: This is a compact old‑style Persian clause whose literal order is ‘to ear‑within‑his said secret long’. به + گوش اندَرِش (اندر + ـش = “in/into his inner/close ear”) marks the recipient; گفت is the past tense verb ‘said/told’ (here irregularly listed in the lemma senses), and رازی دراز is the object (noun followed by post‑positive adjective 'دراز' = 'long').
segments.s167-g202: که introduces reported speech/prescription ('that'/'saying that'); بیدار دل is an adjectival + noun phrase meaning 'wakeful/vigilant of heart' where the adjective precedes the noun (common in poetry). باش is the 2nd‑person singular imperative 'be!'; و links the two imperatives; با کس مساز is a negative imperative using the simple negative stem مساز ('do not make/ally'), literally 'do not make (a partnership) with anyone.'
segments.s167-g203: چو is a subordinating conjunction here meaning 'when'; کیخسرو is the proper name; آید is the present/past literary form 'comes'. The phrase به کین خواستن is a verbal‑noun construction 'to want/seeking vengeance' (به + noun + infinitive) giving 'comes seeking vengeance.'
segments.s167-g204: عنانش = عنان + ـش ('his reins'); ترا is the older dative/benefactive form 'for you' (i.e. 'you must ... for him/you must do …'); را is the object/definiteness marker placed after ترا for emphasis. باید is the modal 'must/ought to' and آراستن is the infinitive 'to array/prepare/equip' — together 'you must prepare his reins.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s167-g205: ورا = 'for him/to him' (dative); بارگی here is the poetic noun 'burden‑bearing' or 'bearer' (i.e. a protector/one who takes the burden), used as the complement of the imperative باش 'be!'. گیتی (world) precedes the verb بکوب (imperative 'strike/beat'), showing flexible Persian order where objects/phrases may come before the verb in poetry.
segments.s167-g206: چنان چون is a poetic double simile 'such as/just as'; سر مار افعی = 'the head of the viper' (possessive relation expressed by juxtaposition); به + چوب here is instrumental 'with/by a stick' (به used to mark the instrument) and چوب = 'stick/staff'. The whole simile modifies the previous command to strike fiercely.
segments.s167-g207: از آخر literally 'from the rear/afterwards' or 'from behind'; ببر is the imperative 'take/cut!' (not the noun 'tiger' here); دل is the direct object 'heart'. به یکبارگی is an adverbial phrase 'in one go/suddenly' with به marking the manner ('by/in a single time').
segments.s167-g208: که introduces the result/purpose clause 'so that/that'; او را (he/him + object marker را) is fronted for emphasis, then تو باشی is the verb phrase 'you be' (subjunctive/optative after که — 'that you should be'). به کین بارگی = 'as a bearer/agent of vengeance' (به marking role/instrument and بارگی the noun 'bearing/burden', modified by کین). The whole clause expresses the desired result of the single‑stroke action.
segments.s167-g105: ز یک‌سو (z yek-su) is a short poetic prepositional phrase meaning 'on/from one side'. شدی is the past 2nd-person singular of شدن 'to become' (you became). آتش تیزگرد is a noun (آتش) followed by a poetic adjective/epithet (تیزگرد) that qualifies the fire as 'keen/quick' — adjective follows the noun in Persian. The whole clause means 'you became a keen/fiery flame on one side.'
segments.s167-g106: برافروختی is past 2nd-person singular 'you kindled' (برافروختن). از + سیاووش + گرد functions as a circumnominal phrase meaning 'around Siavash' (گرد here = 'around/about', and از is the poetic preposition 'from/of' that introduces the locus). So this clause says 'you kindled a fire around Siavash.'
segments.s167-g107: ز یک دست ... ز یک دست ... is a balanced poetic construction 'from one hand ... from one hand ...' where دست is literal 'hand' (here 'one hand' ≈ 'on one side' or 'with one hand/one side'). آتش and آب are the two items distributed to the two hands. Repetition of ز marks the parallelism.
segments.s167-g108: به پیش اندرون is an older/poetic locative phrase meaning 'before/in front of the inner (chamber/hall)'. پیل و افراسیاب are the entities placed 'before' that inner space; و simply coordinates. Note word order: preposition به + noun پیش + modifier اندرون, then the coordinated nouns.
segments.s167-g109: بدیدی is past 2nd-person 'you saw'. مرا (here split in the tokens as م + را) is the object 'me' plus the object marker را; روی کرده دژم is a participial phrase 'having made (کرده) my face (روی) sullen/grim (دژم)'. The sequence means 'you saw me having turned my face sullen.'
segments.s167-g110: دمیدی is past 2nd-person 'you blew' (blew/breathed). بران = بر + آن in fused poetic form meaning 'upon that/thereon'. آتش is the object 'fire'. تیزدم is a compound verb/noun phrase meaning 'I (or a) sharp blast/breath' (here rendered as 'I breathed sharply'); grammatically the line juxtaposes two past verbs with different subjects ('you blew' and then 'I blew/breathed sharply') to show consecutive or reciprocal action.
segments.s167-g111: چو here is the conjunction 'when' (چو = 'when/as'). گرسیوز is the agent name, آن آتش 'that fire' is the direct object, and افروختی is past 2nd-person 'you kindled.' The clause reads 'when Garsivaz kindled that fire' (with the action ascribed to the addressee).
segments.s167-g112: از افروختن uses از + the verbal noun افروختن 'the kindling' to express cause/means ('by/from kindling'). مر مرا (older me-form مر plus the regular مرا; the doubling is an emphatic/poetic device) is the object 'me', with را marking the definite object; سوختی is past 2nd-person 'you burned (me)'. So: 'by kindling (it) you burned me.'
segments.s167-g137: ترا is the preposed object pronoun ('you', with را as part of the object clitic), followed by زنده خواهم: زنده + خواهم expresses the speaker's desire/will to keep someone alive ('I will keep you alive'). که introduces a subordinate clause of purpose/result ('so that' / 'in order that'). مانی is a proper name (vocative/subject of the subordinate clause) and بجای begins the prepositional phrase 'in the place (of...)' which the next segment completes.
segments.s167-g138: سر خویش is a possessive phrase: سر ('head') + خوی + ش (خویش = 'one's own') → 'your own head' or idiomatically 'yourself/your person'. گیر here is an imperative form ('take' / 'seize' from گرفتن used as a command). و links the two imperatives/clauses. کسی را is the object ('anyone/anybody' with the object marker را). مپای is an archaic negative imperative (م- + پای < پاییدن/پای گرفتن) meaning 'do not pursue / do not go after / do not follow'; together: 'take care of yourself and do not go after anyone.'
segments.s167-g139: سیاوش is the speaker's name. بدو is a prepositional pronominal phrase ('to him'). گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here used in narrative). کان is an archaic connective roughly equivalent to 'that' (introducing the reported clause). خواب من = 'my dream' (خواب = dream, من = my).
segments.s167-g140: بجا آمد is an idiomatic perfective phrase 'came to pass / was fulfilled' (بجا here with آمد = 'happened / came true'). و links the two clauses. تیره شد آب من: تیره شد = 'turned dark/bleak' (شد as auxiliary/become), and آب من is a figurative expression for 'my fortune / lot / life' (آب used metaphorically).
segments.s167-g141: مرا is the preposed object/experiencer ('for me' / 'to me'); زندگانی سرآید is a verbal phrase where سرآید (سر + آید) means 'to come to an end' or 'to arrive (at an end)' — here 'life comes to an end' — and همی is the archaic continuous/emphatic particle common in epic style ('indeed/continually' or simply poetic flavor). The literal order is 'to me life will end.'
segments.s167-g142: This segment lists the afflictions that 'arrive': غم و درد و انده ('sorrow and pain and grief' — a poetic triad). درآید is the verb 'to enter / to arise / befall' in the third person, so the clause means 'sorrow, pain, and grief will come upon me.' همی again is the poetic particle marking the ongoing or emphatic sense.
segments.s167-g143: چنین است is a fixed copular clause 'such is / thus is'. کار سپهر بلند = 'the work/affairs of the lofty heavens' — سپهر (heaven/sky) followed by the adjective بلند ('high/lofty') modifies it; کار is the subject meaning 'the affair/operation'. The order is typical older Persian (demonstrative + copula + subject + modifier).
segments.s167-g144: گهی ... گهی ... is a paired adverbial construction meaning 'at one time... at another time...' or 'sometimes... sometimes...'. شاد دارد literally 'has joy' but here means 'gives/causes happiness'; دارد is the third-person singular present of داشتن used as the main verb. گهی مستمند = 'sometimes (it makes one) needy/poor' — مستمند is a predicative/adjective meaning 'needy'.
segments.s167-g337: بریدی is a past second-person form meaning “you cut/you cut off.” دم مار follows it in the object position: دم (here used as the extremity/tip = ‘tail’) + مار ‘snake.’ The sequence و خستی سرش coordinates another action: سرش ‘its head’ is the object of the following verb خستی (an irregular/archaic form here meaning a removed/taken head); ش is the enclitic pronoun ‘its’ attached to سر.
segments.s167-g338: به دیبا بپوشید is an instruction/imperative-like clause: به + دیبا gives the material/instrumental sense ‘with/in brocade’ and بپوشید is an imperative/subjunctival form meaning ‘cover/wrap (it).’ خواهی here is conditional/volitional ‘if you wish’ (literally ‘if you want’), and برش functions as a pronominal/oriented element ‘on/over it’ (here listed lexically as برش but serving to point to the object already mentioned).
segments.s167-g339: گر is the conditional conjunction ‘if.’ ایدونک is a concessive/uncertain particle (old/poetic) meaning roughly ‘perhaps/if by chance.’ او را is the accusative form ‘him’ (او with the object marker را). به جان is an oath-form phrase literally ‘by (your) life,’ where به has an adverbial/instrumental sense and جان = ‘life/being.’ زینهار is an interjection/verb meaning ‘beware’ or ‘take heed’ and here combines with دهی in the next segment (i.e., ‘give warning’).
segments.s167-g340: دهی is the present/subjunctive/imperative root ‘you give’ (here part of the verbal phrase ‘زینهار دهی’ = ‘you warn’). من is the first‑person pronoun ‘I.’ نباشم is a negative subjunctive/optative form ‘I should not be / I will not be,’ and بر شهریار is a prepositional phrase ‘before/upon the king’ where بر = ‘before/upon’ and شهریار = ‘king’—together indicating being held to account before the ruler.
