segments.s167-g341: به بیغوله‌ای خیزم is first‑person: به + بیغوله‌ای (‘to a backwater/obscure place’) marks destination, and خیزم is the present/subjunctive/optative form of خاستن meaning ‘I rise/go’ (here ‘I will go’ or ‘I will withdraw’). از بیم جان is an ablative/instrumental phrase ‘out of fear for (my) life’ with از = ‘from/out of,’ بیم = ‘fear,’ and جان = ‘life.’
segments.s167-g342: مگر here functions as ‘unless’/‘except’; خود is emphatic/reflexive ‘self/own’ (here ‘my own’). به زودی is the adverbial phrase ‘soon’ (به used as the adverbial marker + زودی ‘soon’). سرآید is a jussive/subjunctive verb meaning ‘should come/arrive’ and زمان = ‘time’ — together ‘unless my own time should soon arrive.’
segments.s167-g343: برفتند is the simple past 3rd‑person plural ‘they went/departed.’ پیچان is an adjectival/participial modifier meaning ‘twisting/winding’ and here modifies the following nouns. دمور is an older/poetic noun (contextually something like ‘encampments/dwellings/remnants’) and و is the coordinating conjunction ‘and.’ گروی in this position is a collective/agentive form (here ‘troops/bands’ or ‘a group’) — the whole phrase lists what they were twisting/organizing as they departed.
segments.s167-g344: بر شاه ترکان is a prepositional phrase ‘toward/on the king of the Turks’ with بر = ‘toward/on,’ شاه = ‘king,’ and ترکان = ‘the Turks.’ پر از رنگ و بوی is a post‑nominal adjectival phrase ‘full of color and scent,’ where پر = ‘full (of),’ از = the preposition ‘of/from,’ رنگ = ‘color/appearance,’ و = ‘and,’ and بوی = ‘scent/fragrance.’
segments.s167-g17: مرا دل is an older/pre-classical word-order for the possessor + noun construction (literally “to me a heart”), so here مرا functions like “my / to me” and دل = “heart.” پر از + noun is a fixed pattern meaning “full of X.” رای here means counsel/plan and دیدار means the sight/meeting (i.e. longing to see). تست at the end is a poetic/archaic copular form (see note) linking the whole predication; it behaves like a contracted copula in classical Persian rather than the modern verb form.
segments.s167-g18: دو کشور is numeral + noun (“two realms/countries”). The same construction پر از + رنج و آزار means “full of suffering and torment.” The final تست again functions as a poetic/copular ending linking that predicate to the speaker’s complaint (an archaic contraction/poetic form rather than the lexical verb given in the provided senses).
segments.s167-g19: ز نالندگی is a prepositional phrase ‘from/because of lamentation’ (ز = “from/because of” in poetic style). چون + clause here has the value “as/when,” so چون سبکتر شود = “when it becomes lighter.” سبکتر is the comparative form of سبک (“light” in weight/metaphorically less heavy), and شود is the classical third-person singular present/future-like ‘becomes/shall become.’
segments.s167-g20: فدای تن is a stock expression: فدای + noun = “(be) sacrificed for / devote (one’s) X to.” Here فدای تن = “(to) sacrifice the body” or “give one’s body.” شاه کشور is a genitival/name phrase “king of the realm.” شود again is the intransitive ‘become’/future-like auxiliary; the clause omits an explicit subject (classical Persian permits an implicit first-person speaker), so the sense is ‘(I) will sacrifice my body for the king of the realm.’
segments.s167-g21: بهانه مرا is ‘my excuse’ (بهانه = pretext/excuse; مرا = ‘to me’/‘my’ in older ordering). نیز = “also.” آزار اوست is a predication: آزار = harm/distress, اوست = ‘it is his’ / ‘it belongs to him’ or simply ‘it is (for) him’ — the copula ست = است. Overall: “My excuse too is (a source of) his distress.”
segments.s167-g22: نهانم is a pronominalized adjective/noun meaning ‘my hidden [self/inner part]’ (نهان = hidden, -م = my). پر از + درد و تیمار repeats the “full of” pattern: درد is emotional/affective pain and تیمار means care/solicitude (here the beloved’s concern). اوست again uses the copula to attribute those states to the hidden self: “my inner self is full of his pain and care.”
segments.s167-g23: چو + clause = “when.” نامه = “letter.” به مهر here means ‘with affection / bearing love’ (مهر = affection/kindness; the preposition به marks accompanying/characteristic manner). اندر آمد = ‘came in/arrived.’ The short phrase به داد at the end functions as a dative/verb cluster meaning ‘was given to Dād / was handed over’ — here داد should be read as a verbal/transfer notion (“gave/was given”) rather than the abstract noun “justice.”
segments.s167-g24: به زودی = “quickly / soon.” The sequence به گرسیوز بدنژاد specifies the recipient: به + proper name + adjective/epithet; گرسیوز is a personal name and بدنژاد is an epithet meaning “of low birth / ignoble.” Together: “soon to Garsivaz the ignoble.”
segments.s167-g401: کنون (adverb) 'now' comes first to set the time; زنده 'alive' qualifies the (implied) subject. بر گاه + اسم follows as a prepositional/place phrase: بر گاه 'in the presence/at the court (literally: upon the place)' + کاووس شاه (name + title) — Persian puts the title after the name. The whole chunk functions adverbially ('now, alive at King Kavus’s court').
segments.s167-g402: چو ... و چون ... is a paired simile pattern meaning 'as/like ... and as ...'. دستان and رستم are the comparison terms (proper names); کینه‌خواه is a compound: کینه 'hatred/grudge' + خواه (here participial/agentive 'desiring, seeking'), so 'one who seeks vengeance'. The verb/copula is omitted as is common in epic Persian, so this phrase predicates 'a seeker of revenge like Dastan and Rostam' about the (implicit) subject.
segments.s167-g403: جهان is the subject 'the world'. از تهمتن is a prepositional phrase expressing cause ('because of Tahmätan' — تهمتن is an epithet). بلرزد is third-person singular present (classical form with لَرْز root, here with the present stem prefixed) meaning 'trembles'; همی at the end is an archaic/aspectual particle marking continuous or emphatic aspect ('always/indeed'), typical of Ferdowsi's style.
segments.s167-g404: که here introduces the explanatory subordinate clause ('for/because'). توران is the subject. به جنگش نیرزد is a fixed classical expression: نیرزد (third-person singular negative of نیرزیدن) 'is not worthy/is not equal', and به جنگش 'in/for his battle' indicates the domain in which Turan is unequal. همی again adds aspect/emphasis. The clause explains the reason the world trembles.
segments.s167-g405: The first part repeats the simile marker چو/چون: 'چو بهرام و چون زنگه شاوران' = 'as Bahram and as Zang(eh) of the advisers'. Classical Persian often stacks چو/چون to compare to several exemplars. شاوران is the plural 'advisers/counsellors' modifying or appositive to زنگه; word order places the similes before the relative clause that follows.
segments.s167-g406: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause modifying the previous similes ('who/for whom ...'). نندیشد is negative present third-person (ن + اندیشد) meaning 'he does not think' or 'he is not concerned/does not consider' — in context it carries the sense of 'does not fear/does not mind'. از + گرز کنداوران is a genitive-like construction 'of the mace(s) of the kandāv̄aran (literally 'excavators/investigators')'; together 'نندیشد از گرز کنداوران' means roughly 'he does not take heed of / does not fear the maces of the kandāv̄aran.'
segments.s167-g407: همان 'that same' is a demonstrative emphatic; گیو is the proper name (subject). کز is an archaic contraction (که + از) introducing a causal/relative phrase 'who/from whom' — here 'who, from (i.e. because of) his fear'. بیم او 'his fear' is the cause; روز جنگ 'on the day of battle' is a temporal adverbial placed after the causal phrase. The whole segment is a relative clause describing Giv's behavior in battle.
segments.s167-g408: همی is the habitual/continuous aspect marker; the object 'چرم روباه' (fox-skin/leather) precedes the verb پوشد (classical present: 'he puts on / wears'), following the classical Persian SOV tendency. پلنگ at the end names the metaphorical subject 'leopard' (either appositive to Giv or an epithet); classical verse can place the epithet after the verb for emphasis, so the sense is 'the (leopard-like) one puts on the fox's hide' — i.e. a brave man masking himself in fear.
segments.s167-g457: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” برگرددت is the verb phrase برگردد + enclitic ـت (to you), literally “returns to you” (present/subjunctive form used for a future/conditional time in poetry). روز ("day") is the subject/time noun, and یار is the vocative/address (“my friend” or “O friend”); توام is a predicative form (poetic) meaning “I am with you”/“I belong to you” (the copula + suffix contracted into توام). The whole segment reads as a temporal clause: “When the day returns to you, (I) am/shall be your companion.”,
segments.s167-g458: بگاه is an adverbial/temporal word "at that time/then." چرا in this poetic context is the archaic verb “to graze” (not the interrogative “why”); it introduces an action-image tied to مرغزار (“meadow, pasture”). مرغزار is the place-word “meadow/pasture.” توام again is the predicative poetic form “I am (with/for you).” The clause thus presents a figurative scene tied to the prior temporal clause: “at that hour (I) graze/act in the meadow (i.e. become a meadow or pasture).”,
segments.s167-g459: کنون is the adverb “now.” پیش here means “in front of / before” (positionally, in the presence of). گرسیوز is the proper name Garsivaz. اندر is a locative/into preposition meaning “in/among/into,” and دوان is a verbal active participle or past-form of دویدن (“ran/rushed”). Together this gives a circumstantial phrase: “Now, rushing in before Garsivaz / in Garsivaz’s presence.”,
segments.s167-g460: پیاده is the adverb/adjective “on foot” (here describing their manner). چنین is a demonstrative/adverb “thus/so.” خوار is an adjective “humiliated/despised.” و is the coordinating conjunction “and.” تیره‌روان is a compound adjective meaning “dark‑faced/gloomy” or “ill‑fated.” The string describes their appearance and mode: “on foot, so abject and gloomy-faced.”,
segments.s167-g461: نبینم is the first‑person singular negative present “I do not see” (نبینم = ن + بینم). همی is a frequentative/continuative particle (here reinforcing the verb; often gives an ongoing or emphatic sense). یار is the addressee “friend.” با خود کسی literally “with himself/with you someone” — in context it means “anyone accompanying you” (کسی = “anyone”). The clause thus reads “I see no one with you, friend.”,
segments.s167-g462: که here is the relative pronoun “who/that,” introducing a relative clause modifying کسی. بخروشدی is an aorist/subjunctive/optative poetic form of بخروشیدن (to make a great outcry / to rage/weep loudly) — here “would have cried/wailed.” زار in this collocation is a manner adverb/adjective meaning “pitiably/writhing/lamenting” (i.e., “lamentingly”). بر من means “for me” or “against me” depending on sense; here it is “on my behalf.” بسی is an adverb “greatly / much.” Put together: “who would have wailed pitifully on my behalf a great deal.”,
segments.s167-g463: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” از … شهر و ز لشکر is a compound prepositional phrase “from the city and from the host/army,” where ز is the poetic variant of از. اندر گذشت is a verbal phrase: گذشت is the past tense “passed,” and اندر here completes the sense “passed through/into/among.” Together: “When he passed out of the city and through the army/host.”,
segments.s167-g464: کشانش is the verbal-noun + object suffix from کشانیدن/کشاندن meaning “dragging him” (کشان + ـش = “his/them/him being dragged”). ببردند is the past plural “they took/carried” (ببردند). بر سوی دشت is the directional phrase “toward the plain” (بر سوی = toward, دشت = plain). Altogether: “they dragged him off and carried him toward the plain.”,
segments.s167-g65: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed poetic sequence meaning “thus gave (a) reply” with verb (داد) following the adverb (چنین). The following که introduces reported speech, and که‌ای = که + ای is a poetic/archaic way to address someone: “that: O…”. خوبروی is a vocative epithet “fair‑faced.”
segments.s167-g66: This clause literal order is prepositional phrase + noun + verb + experiencer + object: به توران زمین شد مرا آب روی = “to the land of Turan became (for) me the water of the face.” شد is past “became” used to mean something came to pass (i.e. my tears fell); مرا (split into م + را in the edition) marks the affected person “me” with را as the object/definite marker; آبِ روی is a genitive phrase “water of the face” i.e. tears.
segments.s167-g67: بدین سان is an idiom “in this manner/so,” here split into morphemes in the edition (ب as prefix + دین + سان). که introduces the subordinate clause “that …”; گفتار is the subject “speech/words.” گرسیوزست is a fused/poetic form (here functioning as the predicate, indicating relation to Garsivaz); the construction reports whose words they are.
segments.s167-g68: ز پرگار بهره مرا مرکزست literally “from the compass (پرگار) my share (بهره) is the center (مرکزست).” ز is the poetic preposition “from,” پرگار is literal “compass” used metaphorically, بهره = “portion/lot,” م + را again marks the possessor/affected (“my”/“me”), and مرکزست (مرکز + ست) is the archaic/poetic predicate “is the center.”
segments.s167-g69: Subject + past verb + object + adverbial: فرنگیس (subject) بگرفت (simple past “took/seized”) گیسو (object “lock of hair”), با/به دست here indicates manner/instrument “in (her) hand.” Note the past tense and the concrete physical action.
segments.s167-g70: گل ارغوان را is the definite direct object “the crimson flower” (را marks definiteness/object). به فندق here indicates the instrument or means “with/by a hazelnut,” and بخست is a past verb “tied/fastened/closed” (poetic past form with the ب- prefix).
segments.s167-g71: پر از خون شد = “became full of blood”: پر از (full of) + noun (خون) + شد (past “became”). آن بسد مشک‌بوی functions as an appositive/epithet: آن “that” with بسد (here used as an intensifying/elliptical element) and مشک‌بوی “musk‑scented,” a compound adjective describing the referent. The line uses poetic apposition and compressed word order.
segments.s167-g72: This is a parallel, coordinated structure repeating پر از (“full of”) twice: پر از آب چشم = “full of the water of the eye” (آب چشم = tears), و = “and”, پر از گرد روی = “full of dust upon the face” (گرد + روی = dust on the face). The repetition emphasizes multiple afflictions (blood, tears, dust).
segments.s167-g425: بدو = 'to him' (indirect object). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical narrative use) introducing direct speech. برگرد is an imperative 'Return!' or 'Go back!'. و links this imperative to the following admonition. ایدر = 'here'. مپای is an archaic negative-imperative construction meaning 'do not remain/stand' (negative m- prefix + verb sense ‘to stand/remain’), so ایدر مپای = 'do not stay here.'
segments.s167-g426: چه دانی = 'what do you know' (چه interrogative + دانی present/past-second-person form 'you know' or 'you can tell'). کزین = compound كِ + زِ + این 'from this' / 'of this'. بد = 'bad/evil' (here 'this ill' or 'this misfortune'). م + را: the text shows a clitic m (manuscript variant) followed by the object/topic marker را; taken together they mark the experiencer/possessive relation ('for me' / 'as for me') — the line reads like 'for me' or 'regarding me'. چی ست رای = 'what is (ست = is) the رای (rāy = counsel/plan)' — literally 'what counsel is (for) me.' The whole clause asks rhetorically what the addressee could possibly know about the plan this misfortune holds for the speaker.
segments.s167-g427: به کاخ بلندش = prepositional phrase 'in/at his lofty palace' (به + noun + possessive -ش). یکی خانه بود = 'there was a house' (یکی = 'a certain/one', خانه = 'house', بود = past existential 'was'). Word order places the location first, then the existential clause.
segments.s167-g428: فرنگیس = proper name (subject/topic). زان خانه = 'from that house' / 'in that house' (زان = archaic 'from that' / 'of that', here indicating relation to the house). بیگانه بود = 'was a stranger' / 'was foreign' (بیگانه adjective + بود past copula). The arrangement is subject — oblique phrase — predicate.
segments.s167-g429: مر appears as an enclitic/pronoun form (manuscript/poetic variant; often corresponds to 'm(e)/mā' and can be ambiguous in isolation). او را = 'him' with the object marker را. دران = 'in that' (در + ان = in/within that). انداختند = past tense 'they threw/placed' (transitive, 3rd plural). The core transitive structure is [they] انداختند [او را] دران خانه = 'they threw him into that house.'
segments.s167-g430: در خانه را = 'the door of the house' (در = 'door', خانه = 'house', را marks the definite object). بند = 'fetter/lock/bond' here meaning a fastening or bolt. برساختند = 'they made/constructed' (past, 3rd pl.). Together: 'they made/placed a bolt on the house-door' (i.e. they shut/bolted the door).
segments.s167-g431: بفرمود = honorific/polite past 'he ordered/commanded/said'. پس = 'then' (temporal adverb). تا here introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that' / 'so that' (classical use: بفرمود تا + clause = 'he ordered that...'). سیاووش را = 'Siyâvash' marked by را (topic/object). So this segment sets up the order concerning Siyâvash.
segments.s167-g432: مرآن is an older/poetic compound (mer + ān) that in context functions to point toward 'that' (i.e. 'to/for that'); its precise morphology is archaic and manuscript-variant, so it is here glossed as 'to that' or a linking clitic. شاه = 'the king'. بی‌کین = 'without malice/without grudge' (adjective), و خاموش = 'and silent' (adjective). را at the end again marks the definite object/topic of the ordering construction; overall the clause specifies that Siyâvash was to be delivered to that king, characterized as 'without malice and silent.' Note: the phrase contains archaic pronominal/clitic forms and a classical clause introduced by تا, so word-order and enclitic distribution are typical of epic Persian.
segments.s167-g321: برین here is the fused prepositional phrase بر + این (i.e. 'with/at this'); it precedes the noun it modifies (کینه). بندند is a 3rd‑person plural present (classical/poetic) 'they bind' (from بندن/بستن) and takes کمر ('waist/loins') as its direct object. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly' modifying the action 'bind the loins'. Word order is typical of Persian poetry: prepositional phrase + object + verb + adverb.
segments.s167-g322: در و دشت is a parallel noun phrase ('gate/door and plain') used idiomatically for 'everywhere' or 'towns and plains'. گردد is the impersonal/3rd‑singular present 'becomes' (classical form of گشتن/گردیدن). پر از is the construction 'full of' and کینه‌ور is an adjectival/noun form 'grudge‑bearer' or 'vindictive person(s)'. Together: 'doors and plains become filled with grudge‑bearers.'
segments.s167-g323: نه ... نه is the paired negative 'neither ... nor'. The first clause places the pronoun من before the noun for emphasis: 'I have no پای' (here 'پای' = base/standing/support). دارم is the 1st‑person present 'I have'. The second part (نه پیوند من) is elliptical: 'nor (do I have) a bond/connection' with the repeated من adding emphasis.
segments.s167-g324: نه گردی is 'not even a speck/dust' (گردی = a small particle/atom metaphorically). ز is the poetic preposition 'from', marking source/partitive, governing گردان ('troop/ranks'). این انجمن is the genitive/group being referred to ('this assembly'). The whole clause denies any belonging: 'nor am I a speck from the ranks of this assembly.'
segments.s167-g325: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily/truly' introducing an assertion. که here introduces a subordinate/time clause ('when/that'). پیران is the plural subject 'the elders'; بیاید is a classical present/subjunctive form 'come' (poetic variation of بیایند); پگاه is a temporal adverb 'at dawn' placed after the verb. The clause therefore means 'truly, when the elders come at dawn...'.
segments.s167-g326: ازو is the enclitic/poetic form 'from him'; بشنود is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive 'he/it will hear' (classical). داستان is the direct object 'story/tale'. نیز placed before شاه gives the sense 'and the king also' with an inverted word order typical of poetry (object — adverb/particle — subject): 'the king too will hear the tale from him.'
segments.s167-g327: مگر begins a conditional/exceptive clause here ('unless' / 'except if'). خود modifies نیازت: 'your own need' (نیازت = need + second‑person suffix). نیاید is a classical jussive/subjunctive form meaning roughly 'should/might come' (here used in the conditional sense 'unless ... should require/lead to this'). بدین is the fused form of به + این meaning 'to/for this' (the segmentation in the word list treats دین separately, but in context بدین = 'to this/for this'). The whole clause: 'unless your own need calls for this/compels you to this.'
segments.s167-g328: مگستر is a difficult/archaic/poetic form here; contextually it functions as an instruction 'do not bring/summon/appoint' (the exact morphological analysis is uncertain in the provided lemma list). یکی means 'one / even one person'; تا introduces a temporal/contingent clause 'as long as' or 'while'; جهانست 'the world exists' (literally 'it is the world'); کین = 'hatred/enmity'. Together: 'do not (summon) even one (man) while the world is full of hatred.' I note that مگستر's precise lexical analysis is irregular in the given lemma set, so the gloss treats it as an archaic imperative/negative command derived from context.
